issue_title: Optimize select_for_update, if no key is updated
issue_body: PostgreSQL gives opportunity to optimize locks, using  [`SELECT FOR NO KEY UPDATE` statement](https://www.postgresql.org/docs/9.4/static/sql-select.html#SQL-FOR-UPDATE-SHARE). Django doesn't have this opportunity out of the box, but it will be good to implement it inside this library.

issue_title: Wrap pytest-profiling
issue_body:
https://pypi.org/project/pytest-profiling/ provides some great information, and it is useful to have regular snapshots of it to track perf issues.

But it is information overload for the newcomer and best hidden by default.

issue_title: column_property isn't ignored when reverting
issue_body:
In a table with a column_property, it looks like revert() tries to restore the generated column as well, which it shouldn't do.

```
from sqlalchemy import Column, Integer, String, create_engine
from sqlalchemy.orm import sessionmaker, configure_mappers, column_property
from sqlalchemy.ext.declarative import declarative_base
from sqlalchemy_continuum import make_versioned

engine = create_engine('sqlite://')
Session = sessionmaker(engine)
Base = declarative_base(engine)

make_versioned()

class User(Base):
    __versioned__ = {}
    __tablename__ = 'user'

    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True, autoincrement=True)
    first_name = Column(String)
    last_name = Column(String)

    full_name = column_property(first_name + last_name)

configure_mappers()

if __name__ == '__main__':
    Base.metadata.create_all()

    session = Session()
    u = User(first_name='test', last_name='tester')
    session.add(u)
    session.commit()

    u.versions[0].revert()
```

This throws:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "continuum-test.py", line 32, in <module>
    u.versions[0].revert()
  File ".../lib/python3.5/site-packages/sqlalchemy_continuum/version.py", line 71, in revert
    return Reverter(self, relations=relations)()
  File ".../lib/python3.5/site-packages/sqlalchemy_continuum/reverter.py", line 125, in __call__
    self.revert_properties()
  File ".../lib/python3.5/site-packages/sqlalchemy_continuum/reverter.py", line 48, in revert_properties
    getattr(self.obj, prop.key)
AttributeError: 'UserVersion' object has no attribute 'full_name'
```

Happy to add a filter based on inspect to fix this, but raising here first in case there's a more obvious fix.

issue_title: asyncio - How to properly implement an async pub/sub client
issue_body:
I read through the issue posted by @F483 [here](https://github.com/invisibleroads/socketIO-client/issues/80), which was ultimately solved by making the blocking wait() call on recv() for 1 second.  Given that this is still a blocking call, how would one properly implement an asyncio version (or any type of simple asynchronous methodology) on the code below with limited library exposure? I am having considerable trouble finding a reasonable and simple example for what seems to be a straightforward question. Days of searching. Any help would be greatly appreciated - I'm new to socket development and especially to async frameworks.

Here's F483's example code:
```
import time
import threading
from socketIO_client import SocketIO


class TestSubscribe():

    def connect(self):
        baseurl = "http://v1.livenet.bitcoin.chromanode.net"

issue_title: Emergent - rework to use Apache Arrow for I/O
issue_body:
Hiya Nicolas,

This doesn't really belong as an issue but is more of an FYI that you might be interested in.

As cool as the current implementation is, writing all the data to an html file just doesn't work well, esp. when datasets get large.  [I found the old issues where you discussed and then eventually rejected alternative server side implementations to avoid overcomplicating the codebase.]

Apache Arrow (and Feather files) is an emergent technology that works well with pandas and might help speed the client-side performance.  FYI, (if you're interested), there is currently a Jira open to do a Javascript implementation of Arrow using Typesciprt + Flatbuffers.  https://issues.apache.org/jira/browse/ARROW-541

[Since that impl;ementation doesn't exist yet, it's kinda silly to file this as a Jira, but perhaps this is a placeholder.]

Separately, as an alternative to this conda/python project, have you ever embedded pivottable.js directly in a Jupyter (or perhaps Zepplin or Beaker) notebook?  I can share this if I get it going but if you have something already I'd love to see it.

Best,
Rick

``` python
from autograd import grad
import autograd.numpy as np
import autograd.scipy.stats as st

cdf = st.norm.cdf
gradcdf = grad(st.norm.cdf)
pdf = st.norm.pdf

eps = 1/np.power(10, 6)
fd = (cdf(eps) - cdf(0.)) / eps

print ('at x=0:\ngrad(cdf): %1.6f, pdf: %1.6f, finite-diff: %1.6f' %
       (gradcdf(0.), pdf(0.), fd))

issue_title: Provide config option to toggle the use of models
issue_body:
Introduce a configuration option that toggles the use of models in incoming requests (server-side) and incoming responses (client side).  This is primarily for backwards compatibility with swagger-py 0.7.5 behavior.

When ```use_models``` is ```True```, references to ```#/definitions/{models}``` will be returned as model types. When ```False```, a dict will be used instead.

In ```bravado_core.spec```:

```python
CONFIG_DEFAULTS = {
    "use_models": True
    ...
}
```

issue_title: Incorrect modeling of strncpy
issue_body:
In the man page of strncpy(), it says

If the length of src is less than n, strncpy() writes additional null bytes to dest to ensure that a total of n bytes are written.

But current strncpy() does put addional null bytes to the destination, which makes the different behavior with normal strncpy().

issue_title: Equal resource objects don't necessarily have the same hash
issue_body:
For example,

```
>>> x = ec2.InternetGateway('igw-e98b518c')
>>> y = ec2.InternetGateway('igw-e98b518c')
>>> x == y
True
>>> hash(x) == hash(y)
False
```

This messes up all sorts of assumptions that python likes to make about `hashable` objects, for example when using them in a `set`, and leads to inconsistent results.

issue_title: Retrieving Scalars from Expressions in Python -- slow performance
issue_body:
I profiled the code below (also reported [here](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/dynet-users/y05lip1ULgM)), which looks at retrieving (versus not retrieving) scalar values from Expressions, in this case after running a feed forward network  and softmax transformation several times in a loop. 

As shown in the profile beneath the code, retrieving these scalars takes ages, which is a bit puzzling after looking into the associated Cython and Dynet C code (the issue seems to come at the .get_value(self.vindex) part of the scalar_value function, however the get_value function in Dynet doesn't seem to be doing anything particularly complicated..). 

I'm not sure if this is somehow a problem with how the Cython is written or calling Dynet, or a more general bottleneck related to how the computation graph works (I'm new to dynamic NN frameworks, and still don't understand the underlying mechanics too well). It would nonetheless improve things if this could be sped up in some way.

```python

import dynet as dy
import numpy as np
import pstats
import cProfile

FIRST_HALF_VECTOR = np.ndarray((300,1000),dtype='d')
COMPLETE = np.ndarray((100,1300),dtype='d')

M = dy.Model()

## embeddings
E = M.add_lookup_parameters((20,500))

### hidden layers
HLAYER = M.add_parameters((500,1500))
HBIAS = M.add_parameters(500)

## output 
OLAYER = M.add_parameters((1000,500))
OBIAS  = M.add_parameters(1000) 

def wo_scalar():
    """Run the network without mapping the desired output to a scalar
    """
    for i in range(300):
        first_half = FIRST_HALF_VECTOR[i]

        for j in range(20):
            dy.renew_cg()

            ## first part of vector 
            first_expr = dy.inputVector(first_half)
            
            ## second half expressions
            second_expr = dy.lookup(E,j)

            ## total representation
            rep = dy.concatenate([first_expr,second_expr])

            ## out
            hidden  = dy.parameter(HLAYER)
            hbias   = dy.parameter(HBIAS)
            outbias = dy.parameter(OBIAS)
            olayer  = dy.parameter(OLAYER)

            out = olayer*(dy.tanh(hidden*rep)+hbias)+outbias
            sm = dy.pick(dy.softmax(out),j)

def w_scalar():
    """Run the network and find the corresponding scalar value 
    """
    for i in range(300):
        first_half = FIRST_HALF_VECTOR[i]
        for j in range(20):
            
            dy.renew_cg()

            ## first part of vector 
            first_expr = dy.inputVector(first_half)
            
            ## second half expressions
            second_expr = dy.lookup(E,j)

            ## total representation
            rep = dy.concatenate([first_expr,second_expr])

            ## out
            hidden  = dy.parameter(HLAYER)
            hbias   = dy.parameter(HBIAS)
            outbias = dy.parameter(OBIAS)
            olayer  = dy.parameter(OLAYER)

            out = olayer*(dy.tanh(hidden*rep)+hbias)+outbias
            sm = dy.pick(dy.softmax(out),j)
            sm.scalar_value()

if __name__ == "__main__":

    ## do not retrieve scalar value from output  
    cProfile.runctx("wo_scalar()",globals(),locals(),"Profile1.prof")
    s = pstats.Stats("Profile1.prof")
    s.strip_dirs().sort_stats("time").print_stats()

    ## get the scalar value from output 
    cProfile.runctx("w_scalar()",globals(),locals(),"Profile2.prof")
    s = pstats.Stats("Profile2.prof")
    s.strip_dirs().sort_stats("time").print_stats()

```

Here's the profiler output:

issue_title: `_RNNBuilder` does not support multiple initial_states in ONE computational graph.
issue_body:
Now initial_state is considered as the member variable (`_init_state` ) of  `_RNNBuilder` instances, which means that only one initial state could be used in one computational graph using the same RNNBuilder. 
 `_init_state`  is declared [here](https://github.com/clab/dynet/blob/master/python/_dynet.pyx#L3212), used [here](https://github.com/clab/dynet/blob/master/python/_dynet.pyx#L3334) and [here](https://github.com/clab/dynet/blob/master/python/_dynet.pyx#L3357).

But what if we want to model two different sentences using the same RNN simultaneously but conditioned on different situations (initial states)?

Here is my idea.
1. Remove `_init_state` from `_RNNBuilder` as its member variable, 
2. Let the functional `initial_state` be a simple function to produce a specified or unspecified `RNNState` whose shape matches the RNNBuilder

issue_title: BaseSearchCV throws IndexError for particular sized optional arguments to BaseSearchCV.fit
issue_body:
I'm opening this issue instead of https://github.com/dask/dask-searchcv/issues/74 because of #241.

For certain sized optional arguments to `estimator.fit`, dask-searchcv throws an `IndexError`. It looks like it's trying to index the optional array with the number of examples.

Minimal working example:

``` python
from sklearn.base import BaseEstimator
import numpy as np
import dask_searchcv as dcv
from sklearn.datasets import make_classification
import pytest


class Dummy(BaseEstimator):
    def __init__(self, alpha=0):
        pass

    def fit(self, X, y, classes=None):
        return self

    def score(self, X, y):
        return 1


if __name__ == "__main__":
    X, y = make_classification(n_samples=25,
                               n_classes=2, random_state=0)

    clf = Dummy()
    grid = {'alpha': np.logspace(-3, 0)}
    classes = np.linspace(0, 1, num=24)

    gs = dcv.RandomizedSearchCV(clf, grid)

    with pytest.raises(IndexError):
        gs.fit(X, y, classes=classes)

    gs.fit(X, y)
```



Exceptions are raised only when `len(classes) >= 25`. As expected, the pass with scikit-learn and exceptions are also throw with dask_searchcv.GridSearchCV.

I ran into this issue while integrating #72.

Traceback when the pytest check is removed:

```

    result = _execute_task(task, data)

issue_title: Adding process to the Procfile for a pre-existing app does not create it
issue_body:
Creating an application with the following Procfile:

    web: bundle exec unicorn -p $PORT -c ./config/unicorn.rb

Pushing it, then adding another process, thus making up the Procfile:

    web: bundle exec unicorn -p $PORT -c ./config/unicorn.rb
    rates: bash -c "while true; do bundle exec rake currency_reactor:update_cross_rates; sleep 43200; done"

... and pushing again does not create another process, ```deis info``` still reports only ```web```.

Is there any specific way to add a process for an already existing application?

issue_title: Stress testing results in client Error: invalid character '<' looking for beginning of value
issue_body:
While testing Deis v1.10 in advance of tomorrow's release, I've been stress testing scaling apps with the following:
```console
$ for i in $(seq 1 5); do deis scale web=10; sleep 3; deis scale web=0; sleep 3; done
```

Invariably, one of the scale operations will fail like:
```
=== steely-original Processes
Scaling processes... but first, coffee!
o..!    WARNING: Client and server API versions do not match. Please consider upgrading.
!    Client version: 1.6
!    Server version:
Error: invalid character '<' looking for beginning of value
Scaling processes... but first, coffee!
...!    WARNING: Client and server API versions do not match. Please consider upgrading.
!    Client version: 1.6
!    Server version:
Error: invalid character '<' looking for beginning of value
Scaling processes... but first, coffee!
...!    WARNING: Client and server API versions do not match. Please consider upgrading.
!    Client version: 1.6
!    Server version:
Error: invalid character '<' looking for beginning of value
```

The controller logs show:
```
Aug 31 17:11:51 ip-10-21-1-48.us-west-1.compute.internal sh[2750]: Error:  501: All the given peers are not reachable (Tried to connect to each peer twice and failed) [0]
Aug 31 17:11:53 ip-10-21-1-48.us-west-1.compute.internal sh[2750]: Error:  501: All the given peers are not reachable (Tried to connect to each peer twice and failed) [0]
Aug 31 17:11:56 ip-10-21-1-48.us-west-1.compute.internal sh[2750]: Error:  501: All the given peers are not reachable (Tried to connect to each peer twice and failed) [0]
Aug 31 17:12:11 ip-10-21-1-48.us-west-1.compute.internal sh[2750]: Error:  501: All the given peers are not reachable (Tried to connect to each peer twice and failed) [0]
```

Turning on debugging shows that it's `etcdctl` in the publish loop that's returning the errors:
```
Sep 01 01:41:34 deis-02 sh[21034]: + etcdctl --no-sync -C 172.17.8.101:4001 set /deis/controller/host 172.17.8.101 --ttl 20
Sep 01 01:41:36 deis-02 sh[21034]: Error:  501: All the given peers are not reachable (Tried to connect to each peer twice and failed) [0]
Sep 01 01:41:36 deis-02 sh[21034]: + etcdctl --no-sync -C 172.17.8.101:4001 set /deis/controller/port 8000 --ttl 20
Sep 01 01:41:38 deis-02 sh[21034]: Error:  501: All the given peers are not reachable (Tried to connect to each peer twice and failed) [0]
```

I added a debug curl to try hitting etcd before `etcdctl` runs:
```diff
diff --git a/controller/bin/boot b/controller/bin/boot
index 1264431..c4847f4 100755
--- a/controller/bin/boot
+++ b/controller/bin/boot
@@ -114,6 +114,9 @@ if [[ ! -z $EXTERNAL_PORT ]]; then

        # while the port is listening, publish to etcd
        while [[ ! -z $(netstat -lnt | awk "\$6 == \"LISTEN\" && \$4 ~ \".$PORT\" && \$1 ~ \"$PROTO.?\"") ]] ; do
+               if [[ $DEBUG ]] ; then
+                       curl -v http://"$ETCD"/v2/stats/self
+               fi
                etcdctl --no-sync -C "$ETCD" set "$ETCD_PATH/host" "$HOST" --ttl "$ETCD_TTL" >/dev/null
                etcdctl --no-sync -C "$ETCD" set "$ETCD_PATH/port" "$EXTERNAL_PORT" --ttl "$ETCD_TTL" >/dev/null
                sleep $((ETCD_TTL/2)) # sleep for half the TTL
diff --git a/deisctl/units/deis-controller.service b/deisctl/units/deis-controller.service
index 76ea84c..131ee2a 100644
--- a/deisctl/units/deis-controller.service
+++ b/deisctl/units/deis-controller.service
@@ -9,7 +9,7 @@ EnvironmentFile=/etc/environment
 TimeoutStartSec=20m
 ExecStartPre=/bin/sh -c "IMAGE=`/run/deis/bin/get_image /deis/controller` && docker history $IMAGE >/dev/null 2>&1 || docker pull $IMAGE"
 ExecStartPre=/bin/sh -c "docker inspect deis-controller >/dev/null 2>&1 && docker rm -f deis-controller || true"
-ExecStart=/bin/sh -c "IMAGE=`/run/deis/bin/get_image /deis/controller` && docker run --name deis-controller --rm -p 8000:8000 -e EXTERNAL_PORT=8000 -e HOST=$COREOS_PRIVATE_IPV4 -v /var/run/fleet.sock:/var/run/fleet.sock -v /var/lib/deis/store:/data $IMAGE"
+ExecStart=/bin/sh -c "IMAGE=`/run/deis/bin/get_image /deis/controller` && docker run --name deis-controller --rm -p 8000:8000 -e DEBUG=1 -e EXTERNAL_PORT=8000 -e HOST=$COREOS_PRIVATE_IPV4 -v /var/run/fleet.sock:/var/run/fleet.sock -v /var/lib/deis/store:/data $IMAGE"
 Restart=on-failure
 RestartSec=5
```

Interestingly, after adding this, I see no more errors on scaling...

It should also be noted that @bacongobbler has reported seeing this on AWS, except that he sees errors from confd:

```
Aug 31 17:12:01 ip-10-21-2-166.us-west-1.compute.internal sh[4063]: 2015-08-31T17:12:01Z 43da3b5b8116 confd[145]: ERROR template: confd_settings.py:31:58: executing "confd_settings.py" at <getv "/deis/registry...>: error calling getv: key does not exist
Aug 31 17:12:06 ip-10-21-2-166.us-west-1.compute.internal sh[4063]: 2015-08-31T17:12:06Z 43da3b5b8116 confd[145]: ERROR template: confd_settings.py:31:58: executing "confd_settings.py" at <getv "/deis/registry...>: error calling getv: key does not exist
Aug 31 17:12:11 ip-10-21-2-166.us-west-1.compute.internal sh[4063]: 2015-08-31T17:12:11Z 43da3b5b8116 confd[145]: ERROR template: confd_settings.py:31:58: executing "confd_settings.py" at <getv "/deis/registry...>: error calling getv: key does not exist
Aug 31 17:12:16 ip-10-21-2-166.us-west-1.compute.internal sh[4063]: 2015-08-31T17:12:16Z 43da3b5b8116 confd[145]: ERROR template: confd_settings.py:31:58: executing "confd_settings.py" at <getv "/deis/registry...>: error calling getv: key does not exist
Aug 31 17:12:21 ip-10-21-2-166.us-west-1.compute.internal sh[4063]: 2015-08-31T17:12:21Z 43da3b5b8116 confd[145]: ERROR template: confd_settings.py:31:58: executing "confd_settings.py" at <getv "/deis/registry...>: error calling getv: key does not exist
```

However, by all accounts, etcd is perfectly healthy:
```
{"leader":"b1f111df05fbb9e4","followers":{"51f357c8f5cd5cfd":{"latency":{"current":0.003137,"average":0.0028968183975771607,"standardDeviation":0.008428153523456659,"minimum":2.4e-05,"maximum":1.247249},"counts":{"fail":0,"success":206038}},"e91f26b269e2c668":{"latency":{"current":0.001411,"average":0.002230519207342113,"standardDeviation":0.007685404814379259,"minimum":1e-06,"maximum":1.127447},"counts":{"fail":0,"success":206041}}}}
```

Why are clients seeing this error?

issue_title: Getting 500 error when pulling large Docker image
issue_body:
Sometime I get 500 error when I pulling large Docker image by `deis pull` ``` $ deis create insight-bitcoin Creating Application... done, created insight-bitcoin fatal: Not a git repository (or any of the parent directories): .git To replace the existing git remote entry, run: git remote rename deis deis.old && deis git:remote -a insight-bitcoin Error: exit status 128 ``` `exit status 128` is expected. ``` $ deis pull monacoex/insight:bitcoin -a insight-bitcoin Creating build... Error: 500 INTERNAL SERVER ERROR

## What version am I running?

issue_title: Is it possible to move the .buildozer folder somewhere else?
issue_body:
I have a Python 2 virtual environment where I installed kivy and buildozer. My virtualenv is kept separated from the project folder in a dedicated folder in `~/.virtualenvs`. I tried buildozer today and I noticed that it created two folders: 1) `~/.buildozer` , and 2) `PROJ/.buildozer` , where `PROJ` is the project directory. Is it possible to move `PROJ/.buildozer` somewhere else?

Rationale: I like keeping my project folders in Dropbox, so they are accessible on all my machines. However, `PROJ/.buildozer` is currently 251 MB (too big). I cannot move it somewhere else and put a symbolic link on it because Dropbox doesn't treat symlinks correctly and pulls in the linked folders :(

I think one should be able to set the location of this folder in the `buildozer.spec` file.

issue_title: Kivy can't get the keyboard height with SDL2
issue_body:
This is partly a Kivy issue (it needs some code to check this), but SDL2 doesn't expose the keyboard height. Probably it will be necessary to add code to the java bootstrap, as was done in the pygame bootstrap.

SDL2 does have an option to mark a textinput location, whereupon it will handle the keyboard shifting itself, but (although we could expose this) it isn't as flexible as kivy's existing options.

I have the user-item interactions matrix and item_features matrix created for the items viewed as training data. I train the model using:

`

 `model_si.fit(X_train, item_features=item_features_concat, epochs=optimal_epochs)`

            a.  who were not in the training data but I have the list of items (say a total of 500 items) these users have viewed 
            b. I extract the item_features for these 500 items, say test_data_item_features

issue_body:
Some of the operators (e.g. OptimizedRNN, see #149) require several inputs that can be independently present or absent.

Possible solution: allow some of the positional inputs to be 'None' in the graph with inputs represented as empty string in the proto. Schema checker would enforce that they can appear only after the MinArgs provided.

If not objections - I can cut the diff.

issue_title: Docker container is too old
issue_body:
The ONNX container here seems too old: https://hub.docker.com/r/onnx/onnx-docker/

It would be great if the docker build was automatic. Perhaps it's because the Dockerfile is stored in a repo that is not under onnx: https://github.com/houseroad/dockerfiles

issue_title: Incorrect backend test for LogSoftmax
issue_body: The model.onnx at https://github.com/onnx/onnx/tree/master/onnx/backend/test/data/pytorch-converted/test_log_softmax_lastdim has `axis=-1`, which has no meaning in the spec for LogSoftmax.

issue_title: Model checker should check if every node has a valid name
issue_body: Every node must have a valid name, which cannot be empty.

- pipenv run python -c "import django; print django.VERSION"
- "pipenv run ./manage.py test --verbosity=2 brambling"

issue_title: Regression with setuptools 27.2.0 on appveyor with Windows + Python 3.5 + numpy 1.11.1
issue_body:
Hi,

We have a regression on Windows + Python 3.5 + numpy 1.11.1 on our project when compiling with appveyor.

The issue is relative to unquoted path at  linking step:
```LINK : fatal error LNK1181: cannot open input file 'Files.obj'```
See https://ci.appveyor.com/project/ESRF/pyfai/build/0.13.170/job/vrotml5gkymirvad

I try to reproduce the problem with an own Windows machine without success. Then i only have the issue on appveyor + Python 3.5 + numpy 1.11.1

Here is few tests executed on appveyor according to setuptools versions
- 27.2.0 -> fail
- 27.1.2 -> ok
- 27.1.0 -> fail (also fail on python 2.7, 3.4)
- 27.0.0 -> ok
- 26.0.0 -> ok (but fail on python 3.4)
- 25.0.0 -> fail

While i can't reproduce the problem on an own computer it is very difficult to know what's append. But do you have an idea?

BTW, there is a very mysterious message:
```No module named 'numpy.distutils._msvccompiler' in numpy.distutils; trying from distutils```. Maybe there is a problem on relative/absolute import or something like that? But this message is also displayed on setuptools 27.0.0, the version which works.

issue_title: Moving distutils into setuptools
issue_body:
As discussed on distutils-sig, we want to vendor a copy of distutils and have setuptools use that copy rather than the local Python distutils. Long-term, CPython will remove distutils and `python -m ensuresetuptools`.

@jaraco Would you like me to submit a PR for this or would you like to do it yourself?

issue_title: Move `VM Image List` off of GitHub
issue_body:
Notice in the cloud.py, we have a hardcoded path to a file hosted in github. It means every time users execute `az vm image list`, the command will fetch the file from raw.githubusercontent.com. This render a few issues:

1. Why do we rely on a third party to host files for runtime execution?
2. Github is not a file share service. It is a source control service. That means the url we relies on is not 100% reliable. 
3. Github can throttle the access to this file just like they throttle the release files, which will break our command. 
4. Github can be blocked in some subnet or behind a firewall, which will make this command unusable.
  5.  Github won’t guarantee the performance of this file host.
 
I understand that the logic behind this is that this fire will be updated after CLI is released so as to keep to the latest image types list. However, we should have this file publish to a storage account under our control to guarantee the user experience.
 
```
endpoints=CloudEndpoints(
  | management='https://management.core.windows.net/',
  | resource_manager='https://management.azure.com/',
  | sql_management='https://management.core.windows.net:8443/',
  | batch_resource_id='https://batch.core.windows.net/',
  | gallery='https://gallery.azure.com/',
  | active_directory='https://login.microsoftonline.com',
  | active_directory_resource_id='https://management.core.windows.net/',
  | active_directory_graph_resource_id='https://graph.windows.net/',
  | active_directory_data_lake_resource_id='https://datalake.azure.net/',
  | vm_image_alias_doc='https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Azure/azure-rest-api-specs/master/arm-compute/quickstart-templates/aliases.json'),    # pylint: disable=line-too-long
  |  
```

issue_title: [ACS] The Kubernetes get-credentials command overwrites local kubeconfig file
issue_body:
The `az acs kubernetes get-credentials` command is really useful, however, I noticed that it seems to overwrite the local kube config file (e.g. `~/.kube/config`), and I can't seem to find a way to have it simply add the new cluster/context/user info to my existing kube config file (unless I choose to do it myself and not use this command). It would be cool if there was a way for me to do something along the lines of any of the following:

1. Have the `get-credentials` command add to the default config file. This behavior is similar to how minikube seems to work when you initialize a development cluster. It auto-adds the cluster details to your config in a non-invasive way.

2. Provide a mode that simply outputs the necessary shell commands (e.g. `kubectl config set-cluster`) in order to augment the default config file, which allows you to eval it yourself, similarly to how `docker-machine env` works. #1 would presumably be an automated version of this.

3. Write the ACS-specific config file to a specified location on disk, so I could update my `KUBECONFIG` env var and merge together the multiple config files, or explicitly use the `--kubeconfig` flag when using the k8s CLI. Maybe the existing behavior could default to the global config path, but you could override it with a flag.

Maybe the existing behavior is fine in practice, so I'm just throwing this out there for conversation, but it would be cool to have the same simple experience of creating an ACS cluster and connecting to it, without affecting a user's existing config settings.

issue_title: How to change the dtype ?
Unsafe to load untrusted bcolz data?
issue_body:
Hi,
I noticed that bcolz uses pickle to load objects. Does this mean that it's unsafe to use bcolz to open a ctable retrieved from an untrusted party? Since pickle.load itself is unsafe (allows arbitrary code execution) and the creator of the ctable could have put arbitrary objects into it, opening the ctable could result in arbitrary code execution.

Is there any way around this?

Thanks,
Rohan

issue_title: "Security Considerations" section in the docs.
issue_body:
We identified several issues which would be best practices when deploying a Kinto server:

* Use bucket id's that are unique to the group that has access to the collections in that bucket (i.e. don't use a "default" bucket that would make data from different users use identical URLs).
* Restrict the `"access-control-allow-origin"` header if the allowed origin(s) is/are known.
* Send a `"Cache-Control": "no-cache"` header on each response (this does not prevent caching, but it makes sure the browser cache does not leak info without triggering a revalidate and checking authorization with the server first.

There might be more things worth adding to this section.

issue_title: Enable PyUP to track security updates
How to rotate hmac secret ?
issue_body:
Currently, with the default BasicAuth authentication policy, a unique «userid» is generated from the tuple `user:pass` using the hmac secret in the settings.

If the hmac secret changes, the resulting «userid» will differ. Although technically the existing data won't disappear, the user won't be able to access them!

We have to identify every bit that relies on the hmac secret and make sure that we provide a mecanism to change it without consequence for the users.

Regarding BasicAuth, we could just list this among the limitations of this authentication policy.  

Related #297

issue_title: Tests rely on `at exit` behaviour which does not happen until far in the future.
issue_body:
Running `pytest --basic`, I see a lot of heavy stuff created by tests but then left around to be cleaned up by "at exit" cleanup, which doesn't happen until the whole test suite has run.

For example, with --log-cli=DEBUG,

    swift_t.py                 105 DEBUG    [RUNNER] Queue is empty

is logged every few seconds even after all the tests have completed and pytest shutdown is happening - presumably from an earlier swift_t test.

I also see a lot of this:

    2018-05-23 20:34:44 parsl.dataflow.strategy:155 [DEBUG]  Site:local_threads Status:STATIC

... which might be from a lot of DFKs sitting round doing nothing.

Also, a bunch of `ipcontrollers` are left and (supposedly) cleaned up by at exit behaviour (see another issue/PR I have yet to write).

It would probably be better if all of these things were cleaned up more explicitly: with explicit DFK cleanup in each test, and with that DFK cleanup really cleaning up everything it should.

issue_title: A test name with certain characters in it may create undesirable directory names
issue_body:
Currently directory names are based directly on the test names which can cause all sorts of troubles if the test names contain for example a "/". It is likely that many test-names that would work on Linux will not be possible to create under Windows, causing a mismatch in functionality between Linux and Windows.

Possible solutions:
1. Disallow such test case names
2. Escape dangerous characters
3. Decouple directory names from test case names

issue_title: Add a filter-sam.py script
noninteractive-alignment-panel.py should be able to specify checkAlphabet
Add a method to get consensus information for a title
Add a default for --outputDir to bin/noninteractive-alignment-panel.py or make it required
issue_body: `bin/noninteractive-alignment-panel.py` does not have a default for `--outputDir` and the argument is not specified as `required=True`. So leaving it out results in an exception. I think we should make it required, to make the user explicitly say where to put output. If we add a default then it would be easy to accidentally overwrite existing output. It probably doesn't matter that much either way.

issue_title: SanityBot
issue_body:
## Problem
when experts are working on tens of projects at once, it's easy to miss a step or lose situational awareness on a particular project.

## Solution
A SanityBot that periodically looks at the state of a project and reminds the project team about various things that seem off.

## Use cases
These use cases are heavily biased by the design delivery process at B12. While projects are more complex, we'll consider a project with a customer success manager (CSM) and a designer.
  * A CSM has been on a project for some time but has neither completed setting up the project or asked the customer for clarifying information.
  * The project is set up, but a designer has not been staffed to the project for some time.
  * A designer is staffed to a project, but we haven't sent v1 of a design to a customer in some time.
  * We've sent the v1 of a design to a customer, but haven't received confirmation/started iterating with the customer in some time.
  * We've been iterating with a customer for some time with no result.
  * We've done more than X hours of work on a project.
  * A todo hasn't been checked off in some time.

## Other features that are off the table for now
  * Todos should have due dates, and should be snoozable for some amount of time before they pop up (reminders). If Todos could be set as reminders in some time, you could imagine not warning project teams about tasks while there is a snoozed Todo.
  * It would be nice to have a view of all of the Todos assigned to you across all projects, rather than having to click into individual tasks.

## How to surface sanity checks
  * Create TODOs for team members (CSMs) when a sanity check occurs
  * Send a message to the project slack channel. Note that we should get this for free when Todos are created.
  * Indicate tasks that have new sanity checks (Todos?) in the dashboard.

issue_title: Request: a command to run a command under env
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...**
  - [ ] bug report
  - [x] feature request

conda environments are easy to access from shell scripts. But it's not so nice from e.g. some Python code. Ideally, a piece of code that knows the name of an environment should be able to execute easily a command inside that environment.

Let's say I want to run `pip -V` inside a given environment named "foobar". This won't work:
```python
subprocess.check_call(['conda', 'activate', 'foobar'])
subprocess.check_call(['pip', '-V'])
```

What I'm suggesting is to be able to write something like this:
```python
subprocess.check_call(['conda', 'env', 'run', '-n', 'foobar', 'pip', '-V'])
```
... making the `pip` subcommand execute with all the right environment variables set up (such as PATH, CONDA_PREFIX, etc.).

```bash

```
It works

On 4.6 on canary
```bash

```
It fails.

Removing an option from the CLI in a minor version sounds like a no no. Could we instead deprecate (even if the option does not do anything?)

issue_title: conda links in qt5 files even when they have no place being there
issue_body:
I used this conda env file to create a conda environment:

[ana43noverserver.txt](https://github.com/conda/conda/files/977313/ana43noverserver.txt)

It does not include `qt` nor is it an implied dependency.  Once the environment has been created `conda list` agrees: the `qt` package is not listed in any way shape or form.

However it has been linked into that environment and is the biggest single file in my `/path/to/env/lib` directory:

```
-rwxrwxr-x    2 ijstokes  staff    62M Nov 17 11:33 libQt5WebEngineCore.5.6.2.dylib*
```

What's going on?

Here is my FD version of the question.

I have a mystery that requires some help to sleuth out the answer. 

```
 $ grep libQt5WebEngineCore.5.6.2.dylib ~/anaconda/pkgs/*/info/files
/Users/ijstokes/anaconda/pkgs/qt-5.6.2-0/info/files:lib/libQt5WebEngineCore.5.6.2.dylib
/Users/ijstokes/anaconda/pkgs/qt-5.6.2-1/info/files:lib/libQt5WebEngineCore.5.6.2.dylib
```
which suggests to me that `libQt5WebEngineCore.5.6.2.dylib` is only available in the `qt` package

But then the environment I just created:

```
 $ ls -Fla libQt5WebEngineCore.5.6.2.dylib 
-rwxrwxr-x  2 ijstokes  staff  64927480 Nov 17 11:33 libQt5WebEngineCore.5.6.2.dylib*

 $ pwd
/Users/ijstokes/anaconda/envs/ana43noverserver/lib
```

So I have the file (it is big, and I’m trying to reduce the size of this conda environment), however I don’t have the `qt` package installed:

```
 $ conda list -e ana43noverserver | grep -i qt
```

returns nothing.

So why would the file be in my conda environment?

issue_title: confusing error message when boto is missing
issue_body:
If one tries to install from an s3 channel (e.g., `conda install foo -c s3://bar/baz/conda`) while in an environment, this message shows up:  `Error: boto is required for S3 channels. Please install it with: conda install boto`.  Installing `boto` in the environment doesn't actually solve the problem, however, because `boto` has to be installed into the main set of packages (e.g., `source deactivate; conda install boto`).

Perhaps a simple edit to the error message would address this.  (e.g., `Error: boto is required for S3 channels. Please install it with: conda install boto.  Make sure to run "source deactivate" if you are in an environment.`)

issue_title: Package variation that does not include dependencies
issue_body:
We have had multiple requests for `rpy2` in an environment where R is provided outside of the Anaconda ecosystem: i.e. the system owners have installed it by some other means: from source, from installers, from yum/apt.  But they want the Conda version of `rpy2` and they want it to pick up and work with their pre-existing R.

This is a reasonable request, but one which is not easily handled today.  I believe (though haven't tested it) that this can be achieved by one of:

    conda install --nodeps -c r rpy2
    conda install /path/to/rpy2-conda-package.tar.bz2

But this puts things in a funny state: it leaves you with a Conda environment that has installed packages whose dependencies are not satisfied.  There are (I believe) all sorts of ways a future Conda operation will then try to satisfy those deps or get confused about the state of things, when really what we want is to say "this is just how its supposed to be" -- and if `rpy2` doesn't work because some ancient version of R is installed, or it isn't installed at all, well, that we can live with: Anaconda and Conda did its best to do what the user requested.

`rpy2` is just one example of this behavior, but it is a concrete one so I've decided to focus on it.

We have an active client engagement where we are in exactly this situation.

issue_title: offset=0 in RestfulModelCollection is unused
issue_body:
Code is [here](https://github.com/nylas/nylas-python/blob/master/nylas/client/restful_model_collection.py#L6)

It seems to me that the only reason to have `offset=0` is to _prevent_ a call like 

``` py
RestfulModelCollection(..., offset=6)
```

from actually doing something.  However, if a user is motivated, they can still set the default offset in the constructor:

``` py
RestfulModelCollection(..., filters={'offset': 6})
```

So it seems to me that the `offset=0` keyword parameter is in the code just to be confusing.
Of course, I very well could be missing something... :-)

issue_title: Run SetupMultilingualSite after installing PAM
issue_body:
Thats my scenario:
I have a package holding GenericSetup configs like registry.xml including language-settings (which languages are used, default language and negotiation for example). Also i want to install dependencies. PAM and others in this case.

So installing my package does the settings correct and also installs PAM. So far so good.

But the LRF and LIF folders are not created. Therefore i had to go to Plone's control panel -> language settings and hit "save" without any changes done to trigger the creation process of PAM.

I found out that i can trigger that after my package installation by using plone.app.multilingual.browser.setup.py's code
```
setupTool = SetupMultilingualSite()
setupTool.setupSite(<portal>)
```
inside my setuphandler (using GS 1.8.2+ i can use post_handler on my profile's registry)

I don't think this should be in my package as this seems pretty default to me, to get the language folders and all other stuff set, when having my configs and installing PAM, when building a new portal.

My first thought was: why doesn't PAM run this on installation as it doesn't override/replace any existing content or delete stuff during that process.

**plone.app.multilingual**
configure.zcml
```
  <genericsetup:registerProfile
      name="default"
      title="plone.app.multilingual"
      description="Install to enable multilingual content support"
      directory="profiles/default"
      provides="Products.GenericSetup.interfaces.EXTENSION"
      for="Products.CMFPlone.interfaces.IPloneSiteRoot"
      post_handler=".setuphandlers.post_install_default"/>
```
setuphanders.py
```
def post_install_default(tool):
    from plone.app.multilingual.browser.setup import SetupMultilingualSite
    from plone.api import portal
    setupTool = SetupMultilingualSite()
    setupTool.setupSite(portal.get())
```
The other idea by @pbauer: add a second profile depending on the default profile that i can trigger as a dependency in my package then.
configure.zcml
```
  <genericsetup:registerProfile
      name="setup"
      title="plone.app.multilingual (Setup)"
      description="Install and enable multilingual content support"
      directory="profiles/setup"
      provides="Products.GenericSetup.interfaces.EXTENSION"
      for="Products.CMFPlone.interfaces.IPloneSiteRoot"
      post_handler=".setuphandlers.post_setup"/>
```
metadata.xml
```
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<metadata>
  <version>0.1</version>
  <dependencies>
    <dependency>profile-plone.app.multilingual:default</dependency>
  </dependencies>
</metadata>
```
setuphanders.py
```
def setup(tool):
    from plone.app.multilingual.browser.setup import SetupMultilingualSite
    from plone.api import portal
    setupTool = SetupMultilingualSite()
    setupTool.setupSite(portal.get())
```

**my metadata.xml** would look like that then:
```
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<metadata>
  <version>0.1</version>
  <dependencies>
    <dependency>profile-plone.app.multilingual:setup</dependency>
  </dependencies>
</metadata>
```

Any considerations?
Better ideas?

issue_title: git master/branch structure
issue_body:
Now that we have our first release we need to make a decision regarding how to handle the git tree. This was discussed a bit in Sandia-Labs/PVLIB_Python/issues/33, and @uvchik brought it up in #15. 

I always look first to copy IPython and/or pandas. The [ipython system](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/wiki/Dev%3A-GitHub-workflow) is roughly:

* ``master`` should be the next major release. Now that ``0.1`` is on pypi, this would mean that the ``master`` version should be updated to something like ``0.2.0dev``. See [``release.py``](https://github.com/ipython/ipython/blob/master/IPython/core/release.py)
* Branches should be created for any maintenance releases. So, if we're going to bother with a ``0.1.1`` release then it should be developed in a ``0.1.x`` branch.

So far as I can tell, pandas just keeps bumping the version number by 0.0.1 or 0.1 according to the scope of whatever has been merged. Maybe they let some bigger PRs rot for awhile if they're trying to focus on a maintenance release.

In principle I like the IPython system, but in practice, I think that we should go with the pandas system at this early stage. The IPython system probably makes more sense if/when we get to 1.0 and 2.0 releases. However, I don't care too much about this (we just need a decision so that we can update the version file), and I am especially willing to go with the IPython system if somebody else volunteers to be the main administrator of the maintenance releases and development.

Whatever we decide on should be added to the [Development wiki](https://github.com/pvlib/pvlib-python/wiki/Development-information).

issue_title: Name conflict with Open Babel's Pybel
issue_body:
Hi there, I'm a developer of Open Babel and the author of it's Python interface, also called Pybel. This has been around for a while - e.g. a paper back in 2008, "Pybel: a Python wrapper for the OpenBabel cheminformatics toolkit" (https://doi.org/10.1186/1752-153X-2-5).

We're starting to get support requests regarding confusion over "pip install pybel". We have "pip install openbabel" instead, which installs a pybel.py and openbabel.py. You can see where the confusion arises.

We can do a certain amount at our end to avoid the technical problem of a pybel.py versus a pybel directory , but we would appreciate if you would consider renaming your project to make it easier for users to find the software they are looking for and to avoid confusion.

issue_title: Looks nice! Question and idea
issue_body:
Hey,

Thanks for the spaCy integration --- this looks nice! I've actually been thinking about WMD lately.

I'm hoping you can answer a question for me. I honestly have trouble understanding the EMD paper, so I've never been sure I've understood WMD correctly. The following implementation seemed to match results from the pyemd library. I'm wondering whether you can tell me whether it's correct.

```
def word_movers_similarity(doc1, doc2, sim_metric=None):
    if sim_metric is None:
        sim_metric = cosine_similarity

    doc1 = [t for t in doc1 if t.has_vector]
    doc2 = [t for t in doc2 if t.has_vector]
    N1 = len(doc1)
    N2 = len(doc2)
    similarities = np.zeros((N1, N2))
    for i in range(N1):
        vec1 = doc1[i].vector / doc1[i].vector_norm
        for j in range(N2):
            vec2 = doc2[j].vector / doc2[j].vector_norm
            similarities[i, j] = sim_metric(vec1, vec2)
    print(similarities)
    flows1 = similarities.argmax(axis=0)
    flows2 = similarities.argmax(axis=1)
    sim = (similarities.max(axis=0).sum() + similarities.max(axis=1).sum()) / (N1+N2)
    return sim, flows1, flows2
```

All this does is compute a similarity matrix, and then take the column and row maximums, which represent the nearest similarity for each word in the other document. We then take the average of these similarities.

Is this correct? Usually EMD is described as solving this linear system, and I've honestly never taken a linear algebra class...So that explanation leaves me confused.

Secondly: you might be interested in over-writing the default `.similarity()` hook for the document, using the WMD similarity. You can do that as follows:

```python

class SpacySimilarityHook(object):
    def __init__(self, *args, **kwargs):
        pass # Whatever you need here

    def __call__(self, doc):
        doc.user_hooks['similarity'] = self.compute_similarity
        doc.user_span_hooks['similarity'] = self.compute_similarity

    def compute_similary(self, doc1, doc2):
        # Compute similarity here
        return 0.23

def create_pipeline(nlp):
    return [nlp.tagger, nlp.parser, SpacySimilarityHook(nlp)]

def example():
    nlp = spacy.load('en', create_pipeline=create_pipeline)
    doc1 = nlp(u'Example document')
    doc2 = nlp(u'Second example')
    assert doc1.similarity(doc2) == 0.23
```

The `create_pipeline` hook isn't strictly necessary. You could just append the instance to `nlp.pipeline` at some point, to run the callable by default when processing documents.

issue_title: Can WAL-E send WAL and basebackups to two independent places?
issue_body:
The title says it all.  I would like to backup to my cloud provider and also to a site across the Internet.  The thinking is that the cloud provider would be for "normal" backup protection, and the site across the Internet would provide protection against a catastrophic security breach at the cloud provider.  Can WAL-E handle this?

One issue I encountered when thinking about how to do this manually is what exit status to use if one backup succeeds and the other fails.  The best option would be to report failure, but then it would be necessary to keep track of the fact that one backup succeeded and not keep trying to redo that one.

issue_title: fix command line plotting programs for Windows

Here’s more documentation about interactive mode: https://matplotlib.org/faq/usage_faq.html#what-is-interactive-mode

Below is the minimal code I used to debug pmagplotlib.  There are command line switches for -t (use TKagg), -i (use plt.ion), and -p (print an input() call at the end of the plotting).

Basically, it only works to have an interactive plot AND access to the command line (for save/quit/continue):  on OSX, with TKinter backend, with plt.ion, and with an “input()” call to prevent the program from just instantly quitting.  

On Windows, with tkinter plt.ion and input(), the plot isn’t actually filled in and is “not responding”.  If I take out plt.ion, it works, but is “blocking”.  As far as I can tell from the docs, this is exactly how matplotlib is supposed to work.


    import matplotlib
    import sys
    if "-t" in sys.argv:
        matplotlib.use("TKAgg")
    from matplotlib import pyplot as plt

    if "-i" in sys.argv:
        plt.ion()
        print('not blocking')
    else:
        print('blocking')

    plt.figure(1)
    plt.plot([1,2,3])
    plt.draw()
    plt.show()
    #plt.ioff()

    if "-p" in sys.argv:
        ans = input('hi')

issue_title: Test dependency solving resolution on RPM repo copy
issue_body:
When recursively copying rpm units from one repo to another, only the latest units should be copied. The zoo repo provides packages with dependencies that can be used to verify this functionality. Specifically, it provides two version of 'walrus', and 'chimpanzee' depends on walrus; only one version of walrus should be copied when recursively copying chimpanzee.

Here are the relevant pulp-admin commands:
```
pulp-admin rpm repo create --repo-id zoo --feed https://repos.fedorapeople.org/repos/pulp/pulp/demo_repos/zoo/
pulp-admin rpm repo sync run --repo-id zoo
pulp-admin rpm repo create --repo-id new
pulp-admin rpm repo copy rpm --from-repo-id zoo --to-repo-id new --str-eq="name=chimpanzee --recursive"
```

If this is working correctly, only walrus 5.21 is associated with the 'new' repository. If this is broken, both walrus 0.71 and walrus 5.21 will be associated with the 'new' repository.

This test could potentially be used to test dependency solving generally (or perhaps this could be a separate issue). If it's working correctly, the only latest versions of  camel, chimpanzee, fox, squirrel, and walrus will be in the 'new' repo. If the number of packages associated with the 'new' repo after copy is not exactly 5, and the names aren't the 5 aforementioned names, dependency solving is generally broken.

More information can be found in https://pulp.plan.io/issues/1432#note-9.

issue_title: 30 second Failure Detection and Process Failover
issue_body: This is to test a rather large story: ## Sanity Checking Start Pulp normally and perform a basic sync to sanity check Pulp. start Pulp normally. Allow the system to idle for 40 seconds and observe the logs. Verify that no errors are reported while Pulp idles over 40 seconds. ## Upgrade test This is a 1-time manual test on rpm based installation on EL7. This is not a dev test and likely should not be automated. Upgrade to this release from an earlier release of Pulp. Verify that the /usr/lib/systemd/system/pulp_resource_manager.service contains the line: --heartbeat-interval=5 ## Worker Failure Testing With Pulp normally started, look at the output of `pulp-admin status` and verify all expected workers are present. Then kill -9 a specific worker, for example reserved_resource_worker-0 with sudo pkill -9 -f reserved_resource_worker-0. After 30 seconds have passed check `pulp-admin status` verify that the worker is no longer shown. It is expected that the status API will not show a killed worker 30 seconds after the kill occurs. ## Resource Manager Failover Testing Start Pulp normally, but keep the resource manager stopped. Then in one terminal on the box run one resource manager A with: sudo -u apache /bin/python /usr/bin/celery worker -A pulp.server.async.app -n resource_manager@boxA -Q resource_manager -c 1 --events --umask 18 --pidfile=/var/run/pulp/resource_managerA.pid --heartbeat-interval=5 On a second terminal run resource manager B with: sudo -u apache /bin/python /usr/bin/celery worker -A pulp.server.async.app -n resource_manager@boxB -Q resource_manager -c 1 --events --umask 18 --pidfile=/var/run/pulp/resource_managerB.pid --heartbeat-interval=5 Verify that you see the full Celery "banner" with stars in resource manager A. Verify that you see a log statement that resource manager A has acquired the lock. Verify that you do not see any output from resource manager B. Verify that you see a log statement from resource manager B that it tried to acquire the log but could not. Verify that both resource_managers are reported via pulp-admin status Ctrl+C from resource manager A Verify that resource manager B displays the celery banner with stars within 30 seconds of the Ctrl-C Verify that only resource manager B is shown in pulp-admin status ## Celerybeat Failover Testing This can't be done on 1 computer without changing Pulp code due to both celerybeats having the same name. You can do the same test on two machines with one celerybeat on one, and a second on another. I'm going to apply this diff to the code to allow me to do it on 1 machine in two separate terms. ```diff diff --git a/server/pulp/server/async/scheduler.py b/server/pulp/server/async/scheduler.py index d745406..b158386 100644 --- a/server/pulp/server/async/scheduler.py +++ b/server/pulp/server/async/scheduler.py @@ -28,8 +28,9 @@ import pulp.server.logs # noqa _logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) +import random # setting the celerybeat name -CELERYBEAT_NAME = constants.SCHEDULER_WORKER_NAME + "@" + platform.node() +CELERYBEAT_NAME = constants.SCHEDULER_WORKER_NAME + "@" + str(random.randint(1,10000)) class EventMonitor(threading.Thread): ``` Start Pulp normally, but keep celerybeat stopped. Then in one terminal on the box run celerybeat A with: sudo -u apache /bin/python /usr/bin/celery beat --app=pulp.server.async.celery_instance.celery --scheduler=pulp.server.async.scheduler.Scheduler --pidfile=/var/run/pulp/celerybeatA.pid On a second terminal run celerybeat B with: sudo -u apache /bin/python /usr/bin/celery beat --app=pulp.server.async.celery_instance.celery --scheduler=pulp.server.async.scheduler.Scheduler --pidfile=/var/run/pulp/celerybeatB.pid Verify that pulp-admin status shows both scheduler@ entries Verify that the logs contain a statement like 'New lock acquired by scheduler@xxxxxxxx' Ctrl-C the celerybeat that has acquired the lock. Verify that within 30 seconds the logs emit a statement like New lock acquired by 'scheduler@yyyyyyy' Verify that pulp-admin status shows only one scheduler@ entry

issue_title: Clarify confusion around type of context element in ConjunctiveGraphs  and context aware stores
issue_body:
gromgull, 2011-08-20T07:58:07.000Z 
In the conjunctivegraph, the context field is assumed to be a graph with the identifier set to the URI of the context, i.e. this is what happens if you create context like this: 

{{{
        g=ConjunctiveGraph()
        uri1=URIRef("http://example.org/mygraph1")
        uri2=URIRef("http://example.org/mygraph2")

        bob = URIRef(u'urn:bob')
        likes = URIRef(u'urn:likes')
        pizza = URIRef(u'urn:pizza')

        g.get_context(uri1).add((bob, likes, pizza))
        g.get_context(uri2).add((bob, likes, pizza))
}}}

Now g.contexts() returns a generator over some graphs. 

Now, for code working on the store level, i.e. serializers and parsers, there should perhaps not be any graph objects? 

I came across this when looking at the nquad parser, here: 

http://code.google.com/p/rdflib/source/browse/rdflib/plugins/parsers/nquads.py?spec=svn436d7ac34aee1d42f5ce55f2583d61de7dff9033&name=nquads&r=436d7ac34aee1d42f5ce55f2583d61de7dff9033#142

fc3_weight_1 = mx.nd.array(fc3_weight_1)
fc3_bias_1 = mx.nd.array(fc3_bias_1)
sm_label_1 = mx.nd.array(sm_label_1)

args = {'data':data, 'fc1_weight':fc1_weight_0, 'fc1_bias':fc1_bias_0, 'fc2_weight':fc2_weight_0, 'fc2_bias':fc2_bias_0,
        'fc3_weight':fc3_weight_0, 'fc3_bias':fc3_bias_0, 'sm_label':sm_label_0}

    args_grad = {'data':data_1, 'fc1_weight':fc1_weight_1, 'fc1_bias':fc1_bias_1, 'fc2_weight':fc2_weight_1, 'fc2_bias':fc2_bias_1,
        'fc3_weight':fc3_weight_1, 'fc3_bias':fc3_bias_1, 'sm_label':sm_label_1}
#print args_grad['fc1_bias'].asnumpy()

    reqs = {'data':'null', 'fc1_weight':'write', 'fc1_bias':'write', 'fc2_weight':'write', 'fc2_bias':'write',
        'fc3_weight':'write', 'fc3_bias':'write', 'sm_label':'null'}

##training the model
exe = softmax.bind(ctx=mx.context.cpu(), args=args, args_grad=args_grad, grad_req=reqs)
opt = mx.optimizer.SGD(learning_rate=0.01)
start_time = time.time()

exe.forward()
exe.backward()
opt.update(index=1, weight=fc1_bias_0, grad=fc1_bias_1, state=None)

mx.nd.waitall()
print args_grad['fc1_bias'].asnumpy()
```

issue_title: ACCURACY IS USING NUMPY, URGENT FIX
issue_body:
Accuracy is the bottleneck for even a 4000 node neural network. This is a practical job that many people will run and which a GPU ***CAN accelerate***.

But right now the accuracy metric is the bottleneck. That's because it converts everything to numpy, causing a blocking operation at every iteration. Let's fix it stat.

  
```
for label, pred_label in zip(labels, preds):
            if pred_label.shape != label.shape:
                pred_label = ndarray.argmax(pred_label, axis=self.axis)
            pred_label = pred_label.asnumpy().astype('int32')
            label = label.asnumpy().astype('int32')

            check_label_shapes(label, pred_label)

            self.sum_metric += (pred_label.flat == label.flat).sum()
            self.num_inst += len(pred_label.flat)
```

issue_title: Minimum Specification of IO Iter
issue_body:
We now have multiple versions of IO Iter, with some inheritance relation. 
This issue is for us to get a clear specification of interface, possibly via an tutorial markdown on what is needed to create a user defined data iteration. Two things should be touched.
- [ ] Make a clean, minimum specification of the IO Iter, with 
- [ ] Look for the chance of making io iter in a more light weight manner, e.g. via co-routine and yield semantics, instead of use class inheritance.

I am opening this issue for proposals. Let us put proposals of interface here, and discuss possible form solutions for 2

issue_title: Investigate avoiding heap allocation for every aiFace instance
issue_body: The typical scenario for an aiFace is that it contains no greater than 4 indices. It seems a shame that we still must heap allocate the indices for every aiFace. This may be complicated by the use of this construct in the C interface (and maintaining backward compatibility), but perhaps we could look into using a union and/or alternative approach to avoid these allocations?

issue_title: pre-compiled wheels for linux?
issue_body:
The python bindings are currently provided as wheel packages on [Github](https://github.com/google/brotli/releases/tag/v0.5.2) and [PyPI](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/Brotli/0.5.2), but only for macOS and Windows, and for python2.7, 3.4, and 3.5. For other platforms like Linux, a source distribution is provided, but this of course requires users to have a C compiler.

I would like to help setting up Travis CI to also build [`manylinux1`](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0513/) wheels which are portable across most Linux distributions.

I've done this for several other projects using [`multibuild`](https://github.com/matthew-brett/multibuild), a set of shell scripts originally developed for the Python scientific stack (numpy, scipy, etc.), which makes super easy to set up Travis/Appveyor as wheel builders for Linux, macOS and Windows.

Before I proceed working on a pull request, I first wanted to gauge interest and ask for comments.

We have two options:

1) add `multibuild` to the `google/brotli` repo and configure Travis with additional jobs to also build the Linux wheels.

2) create a separate `google/brotli-wheels` repository with its own Travis/Appveyor CI configuration, which would clone the upstream repo as a git submodule, and would only trigger new builds when the submodule is manually updated, e.g. before a new release.

The latter is also the approach recommended by in multibuild's README, and used by projects like https://github.com/MacPython/numpy-wheels, https://github.com/MacPython/matplotlib-wheels
and others.

I'd tend to opt for the second approach, because I see that brotli's Travis matrix is already quite crowded, and adding support for manylinux1 would add at least 8 new jobs (4 jobs cover all the python2.7, 32 vs 64 bit, "narrow" vs "wide" unicode build; plus 4 more to cover 3.5 and 3.6, 32 and 64 bit), thus further slowing it down.

What do you think?

issue_title: pytest-profiling  does not profile fixtures
issue_body:
Consider the following example:

```python
import pytest


@pytest.fixture()
def work_func_fix():
    s = 0
    for i in range(1000):
        s += i * i
    return s


def work_func_test():
    s = 0
    for i in range(1000):
        s += i * i
    return s


def test_example(work_func_fix):
    assert work_func_fix == work_func_test()
```

Apparently, only `test_example` is profiled. `work_func_test` appears in the profiling results, but `work_func_fix` does not.

Is it a design choice? I've been searching but found no reference to that. Or is there a flag I should pass to enable fixture profiling?

We use pytest to test a Flask application. Fixtures create the database, and instantiate and initialize the application. We'd like to profile the initialization process but it does not appear in the results.

In fact, the profiling result in my example above shows a lot of things I don't care about (everything that is not under `test_example`), but not the fixture do I care about.

issue_title: Invoked functions should not accept `Input<T>` as inputs
issue_body:
At the moment, `runtime.invoke` -- for accessing provider functions -- returns a `Promise<T>`.  This feels wrong, since the input arguments may very well carry dependencies, and it complicates composing returned values with other inputs to resource operations, which are usually `Output<T>`s.

It seems this should instead return an `Output<T>` that carries the complete dependency information.

issue_title: Support ownership transfer for `unique_ptr<T>` (move-only holders) from Python to C++ (and back)
py::enum_ does not behave correctly when the underlying type of enum class is char
issue_body:
## Issue description

I was exposing a enum class to python, and this is my code:
``` cpp
// enum class to expose
enum class Player : char { 
    White = -1, None = 0, Black = 1 
};
```

```cpp
// pybind11 wrapper
py::enum_<Player>(mod, "Player", "Gomoku player types")
    .value("white", Player::White)
    .value("none",  Player::None)
    .value("black", Player::Black)
    //.def(py::init<int>()) this line run into another error
    .def("__int__",   [](Player p) { return static_cast<int>(p); })
    .def("__float__", [](Player p) { return static_cast<double>(p); })
    .def("__hash__",  [](Player p) { return static_cast<int>(p); })
    .def("__neg__",   [](Player p) { return -p; })
    .def_static("calc_score", [](Player p, Player w) { return getFinalScore(p, w); });

```

As I was testing the module in python, I ran into such a strange issue:
```bash
>>> int(Player.black)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: __int__ returned non-int (type str)

>>> hash(Player.black)
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
TypeError: __hash__ method should return an integer

>>> float(Player.black)
1.0
```
In fact, similar issues have occurred when I pass the `py::arithmetic()` extra parameter to enum.

Then I checked the inner member functions and discovered these:
```bash
>>> Player.black.__int__()
'\x01'
>>> Player.white.__int__()
'ÿ'
>>> Player.white.__hash__()
'ÿ'
```

So, why does a `static_cast<int>` resulted in a str in python?

**Intuitively,  I change the underlying type of `enum class Player` from `char` to `int`, and:**
```bash
>>> Player.black.__int__()
1
>>> Player.white.__hash__()
-1
>>> int(Player.none)
0
```
Thus, it is the underlying type `char` of `enum class player` that causes all these bugs.

issue_title: Multiple inheritance change seems to break holder type test in some cases
issue_body:
Or at least it breaks my understanding of it. So perhaps all that is needed is a change to the documentation.

The following wrapper:

```py::class_<Mask<unsigned short>, std::shared_ptr<Mask<unsigned short>>, ImageBase<unsigned short>> cls(mod, "MaskS");```

fails to compile with:

```static_assert failed "Unknown/invalid class_ template parameters provided"
    static_assert(detail::all_of_t<is_valid_class_option, options...>::value,
    ^             ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
python/lsst/afw/image/mask.cc:37:92: note: in instantiation of template class```

But works once the `PYBIND11_DECLARE_HOLDER_TYPE(T, std::shared_ptr<T>)` macro is added at the top.
This did not use to be the case with the old `py::base` syntax. Perhaps this can be clarified in the documentation?
I was wondering also if it is perhaps not possible to somehow eliminate the need to add the macro altogether? Failing to put that in is a frequent source of errors.

issue_title: Pause in _retry_read_url should only happens from the second time
issue_body:
Hi,


I noticed in `utils.py` in `_retry_read_url` this

    def _retry_read_url(url, retry_count, pause, name):
        """
        Open url (and retry)
        """
        for _ in range(retry_count):
            time.sleep(pause)
            ...

So an unnecessary pause occured from begining (first request)

we might have something like:


    def _retry_read_url(url, retry_count, pause, name):
        """
        Open url (and retry)
        """
        for _ in range(retry_count):
            if _ != 0:
                time.sleep(pause)
            ...

issue_title: Eclipse recognizes every DICOM set generated by pytrip as the same patient
issue_body:
![matchingname](https://cloud.githubusercontent.com/assets/22157203/23229784/d77fc628-f941-11e6-8fb9-adab14037e9f.png)
Here Eclipse thinks that the pytrip-generated DICOM set of Alinas patient from #245  is the same as the test files sent by Leszek and Niels  in regard to #193 (which is clearly not the case).  
The patient ID1 is always 123456 for DICOM sets generated with pytrip. Therefore Eclipse thinks it is the same patient. This seems to happen no matter what the other UIDs say because clearly they are listed with different names ("tst002" vs "cube"), being the original names of the respective .hed file.

This issue makes it a bit difficult to joggle with more than one pytrip-generated DICOM set at a time, because you have to delete the existing patient first in order to import the new one.

issue_title: incorrect authentication interface
issue_body:
Dear pyxnat

it appears that there is a problem with xnat authentication mechanism.

the mechanism asks for a username and a password, but xnat supports several user databases for
authentication purposes. The internal xnat logic fails if several databases contain same username.
As far as I can tell, this is an internal design flaw of XNAT and one cannot expect this to be fixed.

However, the HTTP FORM POST interface (which is used by web browsers) works correctly.

i suggest pyxnat implements this interface. 
Here is an example how to use it with curl

curl -c COOKIE_FILE
      --data-urlencode login_method=SERVICE \
      --data-urlencode j_username=USER   \
      --data-urlencode j_password=PASS  \
      'http://url.of.xnat.server/xnat/j_spring_security_check'

SERVICE is the name of the authentication database...

Best wishes and thank you for your help

LF

issue_title: Question: start concurrent jobs with time intervals in between
issue_body:
Same example as #926 but instead of worrying about disk space we worry here the download bandwidth balance. The workflow is again pasted here:

```
[1]
input: for_each = ['files_sra'], group_by = 1, concurrent = True
output: files_sra
download: 
  {url}/{_files_sra}

[2]
output: files_bam
run:
 sratools-dump ${_input} ${_output}
```

Apparently we cannot use very large `-j` for step 1 due to band-width limit. But if we use eg, `-j 1` then after download step 1, the `sratools` decryption step 2 will take long time, and the workflow will not download anything. This is a waste of resource. 

So ideally we want something like this:

```
1st file download -> 1st file decryption &  2nd file download -> 2nd file decryption & 3rd file download ...
```

How can we use SoS existing features to achieve this?

issue_title: IOLoop.close(all_fds=True) can raise RuntimeError
issue_body:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/home/antoine/distributed/distributed/utils_test.py", line 435, in test_func
    loop.stop()
  File "/home/antoine/miniconda3/envs/dask35/lib/python3.5/contextlib.py", line 66, in __exit__
    next(self.gen)
  File "/home/antoine/distributed/distributed/utils_test.py", line 109, in pristine_loop
    loop.close(all_fds=True)
  File "/home/antoine/tornado/tornado/ioloop.py", line 719, in close
    for fd, handler in self._handlers.values():
RuntimeError: dictionary changed size during iteration
```

The explanation: `IOLoop.close(all_fds=True)` calls `.close()` on each iterated socket object, and `_AsyncSocket.close` calls `IOLoop.remove_handler()` immediately.

issue_title: Phase processing in python3
issue_body: I have been slowly upgrading to python 3 and ran into a snag central doing phase processing with PyART.  AFAIK, cylp is not compatible with python 3, and neither is PyGLPK (last updated last decade).  I am currently dealing with the pain of installing CVXOPT on OS X (BLAS/LAPACK) using homebrew.  It would be really nice if cylp could be upgraded to python 3 (and support more recent versions of cbc).

issue_title: Flash without resetting the board
issue_body:
Add special `--no-reset` parameter for `mbedflash flash` sub-command, which will skip touching the serial port. This is preferred by the client, because then it can keep serial port open and skip some error handling.

I do not think it is really necessary to [reset](https://github.com/ARMmbed/mbed-flasher/blob/master/mbed_flasher/flashers/FlasherMbed.py#L192) the board before flashing... DAPlink should do what is necessary. Or is it not so?

Client will reset the board after flasher is done. When a power switch is linked to the board, it will use it. It might be an important feature to support e.g. power consumption profiling in early boot. We flash first and leave the target halted, then client launches power meter recording and unlocks the board's MCU.

issue_title: Ability to generate static maps
[cassandra] Queries generated by Session.execute_async do not generate spans.
issue_body:
Queries that are ran by using `Session.execute_async` do not generate spans. 

I'm in touch with @palazzem about the issue on Slack. 

Minimal reproduction, assuming you have a keyspace named `test` with a table, called `a`, with the appropriate fields. 

```py
cluster = cluster.Cluster()
keyspace = cluster.connect('test')
statement = keyspace.prepare('SELECT a, b, c FROM test WHERE a = ?')

def do_some_stuff():
    futures = []
    for i in xrange(100):
        futures.append(keyspace.execute_async(statement, {'a': 1}))

    return [
        c.result()[0]
        for c in futures
    ]
```

```
CREATE KEYSPACE IF NOT EXISTS test WITH replication = {'class': 'SimpleStrategy', 'replication_factor': '1'};
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS test.test (
    a bigint,
    b bigint,
    c bigint,
    PRIMARY KEY (a)
);
```

issue_title: Merge after Dimshuffle fails
issue_body:
I was trying to do an element wise merge between two layers after I did a dimshuffle on one of them:

`dimshuffled_layer = layers.DimshuffleLayer(layer_a, (0, 1, 2, 'x'))`
`merged_layer = layers.ElemwiseMergeLayer([layer_b, dimshuffled_layer], T.mul)
`

But it failed. I looked into the code and it seems that Lasagne does not keep track of broadcastable dimensions. I worked around this by changing [the output shape component of DimshuffleLayer](https://github.com/Lasagne/Lasagne/blob/master/lasagne/layers/shape.py#L269) from
`o = 1` to `o = None`. But I don't know if this break other things.

Should we keep track of the broadcastable dimensions?

issue_title: Sparse input to Lasagne
issue_body:
I'm currently working on a problem that I want to solve with multilayered perceptron, yet the data is very sparse and it would be much simpler if Lasagne had an InputLayer that would accept sparse input. Theano does have the needed theano.sparse module that works with csr / csc matrices from scipy. What is the easiest way to use sparse matrices as an input in Lasagne? 

The only idea I came up with is to reimplement InputLayer + Dense layers so that they would accept sparse matrices and employ sparse operations introduced by Theano, but it seems like an overkill. What would you recommend? Is there any PR that I missed with needed functionality? Would that be interesting for a PR if I implemented the mentioned Layers?

issue_title: Rebalancing not work properly when Monkey patching
issue_body:
**PyKafka version**:  2.5.0
**Kafka version**: 0.10.2

PyKafka can't balancing consumers when using `gevent` and `patch_all()` function.

Looking further it seems there is an issue when pykafka tries to acquire the lock to rebalance on `_rebalance` function in `BalancedConsumer` class.

Here is a code example:
```python
from gevent import monkey
monkey.patch_all()

import logging
logging.basicConfig(level=logging.INFO)

import gevent

from pykafka import KafkaClient
from pykafka.common import OffsetType


def consume(consumer):
    while True:
        message = consumer.consume()
        print('Message: %s' % message.value)


if __name__ == '__main__':
    client = KafkaClient(hosts='localhost:9092')
    topic = client.topics['test']

    consumer = topic.get_balanced_consumer(
        consumer_group='consumer',
        auto_commit_enable=True,
        auto_commit_interval_ms=1000,
        zookeeper_connect='localhost:2181',
        reset_offset_on_start=False,
        auto_offset_reset=OffsetType.LATEST,
        rebalance_max_retries=10
    )

    jobs = [gevent.spawn(consume, consumer)]
    gevent.joinall(jobs)
```
And here is my pip freeze output:
```
gevent==1.2.1
greenlet==0.4.12
kazoo==2.2.1
pykafka==2.5.0
six==1.10.0
tabulate==0.7.7
wsgiref==0.1.2
```

issue_title: eliminate `requirements_test.txt` + add tests to setup.py
issue_body:
yesterday's bad release was due (in large part) to the distinction between `requirements.txt` and `requirements_test`.txt.

briefly, I added a new feature that required a "new" dependency, tried to pip install it, saw that it was already there, and so assumed it was already transitively included in our requirements. but it turns out it was actually one of the *test* requirements (which I have installed, but which don't get pip installed). TeamCity didn't catch this either, because it also installs test requirements!

I failed to catch it manually at the "test pypi test install" step, so that's bad on me, but our release process shouldn't rely on that sort of manual verification (and anyway it could have been a much more subtle bug).

This is admittedly an edge case, but (I hit it and so) I think it speaks to having *ONE INSTALL PATH*. If our CI system isn't testing the same "environment" that's going into our pip package, that's very bad.

(Before we do this we should eliminate the Jupyter dependency from requirements_test.txt)

along similar lines, I think we should distribute our tests as part of the pip package, so that we can TEST TEST our pip install

issue_title: Make a clear plan for Beaker parameter exploration.
Discussion needed on how to best handle non ssl connections
issue_body: Currently all connections default to using https. For systems that do not have https setup, a workaround of setting the host to `http://<ip>:<port>/` is possible, but it seems like maybe we should have a config option and/or flag for setting the protocol. Perhaps call it `protocol` with the default value being `https`?

issue_title: --auto-hsts should disable HSTS if renewal has failed N times
issue_labels: [{'id': 291660534, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyOTE2NjA1MzQ=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20hsts', 'name': 'area: hsts', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 261409142, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyNjE0MDkxNDI=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20renewal', 'name': 'area: renewal', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}, {'id': 291660454, 'node_id': 'MDU6TGFiZWwyOTE2NjA0NTQ=', 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/certbot/certbot/labels/area:%20security%20enhancements', 'name': 'area: security enhancements', 'color': 'c5def5', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Having an HSTS header makes a certificate error unrecoverable on the user end, which is obviously very bad. The `--auto-hsts` option should try to avoid contributing to such situations if at all possible.

It's reasonably common for certificate renewal to be failing for some silly reason, but if that's happening the auto hsts code should notice and disable the HSTS header to mitigate the damage.

Ideally there should be a constraint `N + HSTS-max-age-in-days < 30`, if that's possible.
