issue_title: Documentation cleanup!
issue_body:
We have documentation in too many places.  Let's clean up and deprecate them!

Ones we want to keep:
* reStructuredText in this repo (and on readthedocs.org), in three main sections:
  * including the introduction (README.rst in the top level)
  * a using.rst "user guide"
  * a contributing.rst "developer guide"
* the instruction generator that lives in [certbot/website](https://github.com/certbot/website/tree/master/_scripts/instruction-widget) 
* FAQs on the Certbot website

Ones we want to get rid of:
* the wiki on this repo
* distinctly worded material at https://letsencrypt.org/getting-started

Ones that are in the middle:
* docs that are on community.letsencrypt.org

issue_title: Support "new config file" when no vhost / servername found
issue_body:
Using the Apache configurator on our acme-client test machine, I got a cert for one of my hostnames that didn't already exist in any of the Apache configs. At one point I got a screen that said "We were unable to find a vhost with a ServerName or Address of abcde.crud.net. Which virtual host would you like to choose?"

None of the virtual hosts was really suitable, since I hadn't yet set one up for that hostname on that machine. Ideally we should offer a "new vhost" option that would create a file in the appropriate directory. I realize this is probably quite fraught: How do we know if the new file will be included into the main config; what do we set its documentroot to.

As a stopgap, to make the messaging clearer, maybe we should add language like: "If you haven't yet set up Apache for <host> on this machine, we suggest doing that now and running letsencrypt again."

issue_title: New table style makes it impossible to view data (5.0 beta)
issue_body:
I know this has been raised and discussed before (https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/issues/961, https://github.com/jupyterlab/jupyterlab/pull/910#issuecomment-248599489, and actually the max-width has been increased due to that discussion), but, with the risk of trolling, I would like to raise this again (and opening an issue here instead of at jupyterlab, as with the 5.0 beta it seems more urgent here). 
To be clear: I really like the new table style! My only concern is regarding the hard-coded max-width and hiding of data.

**Problem**: due to the hard-coded `max-width: 150px` combined with `white-space: nowrap`, actual data gets hidden without any alternative way to look at the actual data. 
For me, the notebook is for a large part about "interacting with my data". An essential aspect of this, just looking at what is in my dataframe, is now impossible once you have a bit longer cell contents.

And you very easily get to this limit of 150px, eg timestamps with subsecond digits (common in certain fields), a bit longer species names, review comments, ...

**Possible solution**: My preferred solution would be to rework this part of the table styling to *always display all provided data* (and there are some different options to obtain this, like no max-width, or a max-width but with text wrapping over multiple lines, ...) 
Other way of putting it is this principle: let the library that generates the table control what data is visible, and let jupyter just display it 'unopinionated'.

As I said, the above is my preferred solution because this would let pandas (and other libraries generating tables) be able to control what is actually displayed (as we already currently do with `pd.options.display.max_colwidth`). 
But of course, there can also be other solutions like:

- make this easily configurable from jupyter
- display full data on hover
- ..

To be clear: it doesn't *has* to be my provided solution, but at least I hope you are open to reconsidering this aspect of the table styling (irregardless of the outcome of the discussion in the end :-)).

---

Note: we can override this from the pandas side, by including a style tag with every generated table. Relevant issue is here: https://github.com/pandas-dev/pandas/issues/15379 (and we would consider a bug-fix 0.19.3 release if necessary).
I will put some illustration of the interaction of pandas with the new table style in a next comment.

issue_title: Document the notebook kernel websocket message spec
issue_body:
The messages coming on a websocket connection to the kernel bridge are slightly different than the plain kernel message spec. We should document these differences for notebook server clients.

For example:

1. Messages have a `channel` top-level field to indicate what channel the messages are coming from.
2. The notebook itself sends some messages, like kernel restarting messages.
3. The websocket wire format should be documented.

I'm sure there are more differences. 

CC @minrk

issue_title: Removing the play button introduced in 5.6.0?
issue_body:
I understand the rationale of trying this feature out, but I worry that it becomes a feature that if we keep this change now we will have a harder time deprecating it in the future. We don't have enough information about it to keep it as the new default, and in a day of usage, I find it to be visual noise and frustrating. 

One particular problem with the current implementation is target area for executing a cell in place fills the entire vertical column, but the icon is only at the top.

Alternatives paths forward could be to provide this as an option, or to patch the release taking this feature out and put it back in in `master` to let it bake for longer. 

I feel that a UI change like this is significant and I think that the cost to current users who do not need it is too great for the benefit it provides to those who want such a feature.

issue_title: Salt correction params ignored in hairpin,etc calcs
issue_body:
Hi Primer3-py,

According to the following post, the methods: calcHeterodimer, calcHairpin, etc. should be able to use the tm_method and salt_correction_method, is that something you could make primer3-py support as well?
https://sourceforge.net/p/primer3/feature-requests/25/

Here is a code example showing the issue:

    >>> primer3.thermoanalysis.ThermoAnalysis(salt_correction_method=0).calcHeterodimer('TGAGGCACCTATCTCAGCGA','TCGCTGAGATAGGTGCCTCA').tm
    62.99575324661981
    >>> primer3.thermoanalysis.ThermoAnalysis(salt_correction_method=1).calcHeterodimer('TGAGGCACCTATCTCAGCGA','TCGCTGAGATAGGTGCCTCA').tm
    62.99575324661981
    >>> primer3.thermoanalysis.ThermoAnalysis(salt_correction_method=0).calcTm('TGAGGCACCTATCTCAGCGA')
    59.81988509331603
    >>> primer3.thermoanalysis.ThermoAnalysis(salt_correction_method=1).calcTm('TGAGGCACCTATCTCAGCGA')
    63.07165934075988

As you can see, for the hetero dimer calculations, the salt_correction_method has no influence.

Best, Martin

The children of `pre` tags should not be linkified when `skip_pre` is on ``` diff --git a/bleach/__init__.py b/bleach/__init__.py index 48b6512..4c2dd1b 100644 --- a/bleach/__init__.py +++ b/bleach/__init__.py @@ -300,7 +300,7 @@ def linkify(text, callbacks=DEFAULT_CALLBACKS, skip_pre=False, if node.tag == ETREE_TAG('pre') and skip_pre: linkify_nodes(node, False) elif not (node in _seen): - linkify_nodes(node, True) + linkify_nodes(node, parse_text) current_child += 1 diff --git a/bleach/tests/test_links.py b/bleach/tests/test_links.py index 62da8d1..ae0fba7 100644 --- a/bleach/tests/test_links.py +++ b/bleach/tests/test_links.py @@ -314,6 +314,13 @@ def test_skip_pre(): eq_(nofollowed, linkify(already_linked)) eq_(nofollowed, linkify(already_linked, skip_pre=True)) +def test_skip_pre_child(): + # Don't linkify the children of pre tags. + intext = '
http://foo.com
http://bar.com' + expect = '
http://foo.com
http://bar.com' + output = linkify(intext, skip_pre=True) + eq_(expect, output) + def test_libgl(): """libgl.so.1 should not be linkified.""" ```

issue_title: Create a tool for running a test set based on a given checkpoint and publish the results
Epoch 0 - loss: inf
issue_body:
Following the directions in the README.md for training on the Common Voice dataset, I get:
```
W Parameter --validation_step needs to be >0 for early stopping to work
I STARTING Optimization
I Training of Epoch 0 - loss: inf
```

No custom code.
Using ScientificLinux 7 (basically CentOS 7)
Tensorflow installed from upstream (pip, as instructed), version 1.5.0 (v1.5.0-0-g37aa430d84)
Python 2.7.13
CUDA 9.0, cuDNN 7.0.5
GPU: TITAN X (pascal).

This is exactly what I'm doing:
```
git clone https://github.com/mozilla/DeepSpeech.git
cd DeepSpeech
virtualenv --no-site-packages -p `which python` .ds-env
. .ds-env/bin/activate

export LC_ALL=C
pip install --upgrade pip setuptools requests
pip install -r requirements.txt
pip uninstall -y tensorflow
pip install 'tensorflow-gpu==1.5.0'
pip install deepspeech-gpu

python util/taskcluster.py --target native_client --arch gpu
mkdir -p data/CV
bin/import_cv.py ./data/CV
mv data/CV/cv-other-train.csv data/CV/cv-other-train.csv.backup
perl -ne 'if(!/,\r/) { print; }' < data/CV/cv-other-train.csv.backup > data/CV/cv-other-train.csv

./DeepSpeech.py --train_files data/CV/cv-valid-train.csv,data/CV/cv-other-train.csv --dev_files data/CV/cv-valid-dev.csv --test_files data/CV/cv-valid-test.csv --checkpoint_dir=my-model/cv-chkpt --export_dir=my-model/exported/
```

Changing the learning rate to 0.0001 gives the same output.

Maybe not a bug in the implementation - it could be that the steps in the README are not complete/correct?

issue_title: Hidden Technical Debt
issue_body:
Right now we're at the behest of a project that hasn't pushed out a new release in a while (Immutable), and we're also not currently using it properly -- we have nested elements we're changing in our overall structure that are mutable.

Here's what I'd like to do, ideally -- use regular objects and never mutate them in place (with the assistance of `lodash/fp` or a lot of `Object.assign`).

issue_title: `record`ing non-JSON'able objects
issue_body:
I tried to `record` a numpy array but that fails somewhere in a step to clean the JSON. I think the problem is that there is no builtin way to convert a numpy array to JSON.

What is the recommendation for outputting numpy arrays? For example I could imagine that "arrays tend to be too big, so store them somewhere else and `record` the path to it" is one option. An alternative would be to add a step to `pickle`/serialize types that can't be stored as JSON in `record` before dumping them into the notebook.

I'd be up for implementing the latter option but wanted to discuss ideas first.

issue_title: SpatialPooler: inhibitColumnsGlobal() does unneeded reverse sort on returned list
issue_body:
The list returned is the [list of active columns](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L777) (in sparse form), is used in 2 downstream methods:

1. [_adaptSynapses](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L780)
2. [_updateDutyCycles](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L781)

...in the first method, the sparse list of columns [is only used as indexes to iterate over](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L1034); and in the second, the sparse list [is used to create a dense list](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L880) whose indexes indicated by the sparse list are made 1's.

[```return sortedWinnerIndices[start:][::-1]```](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L1440)

**Argument:** I'm thinking there is no reason to sort the list in reverse order? Even if there is some "magical" underlying C++ method which does it quickly, it's still a useless operation and results in cognitive dissonance?

issue_title: Account for chunk-extension values when parsing chunked responses

When ipwb replay parses the chunked responses, it does not consider the potential extensions values per https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc2616#section-3.6.1 and in discussion at https://github.com/oduwsdl/ipwb/pull/124#discussion_r103848851. Check if a semi-colon is present and if so, parse out the chunk size as the first value rather than relying on the entirety of the line.

Transform multiple images/coordinates at once
issue_body:
When image is transformed, associated images / coordinates should be transformed as well.
For example, I want to horizontally flip (img, heatmap, landmark) altogether but not independently.

```
img = transforms.RandomHorizontalFlip()
if img is flipped:  # not possible now
  heatmap = hflip(heatmap)
  landmark_point = hflip(landmark_point)
```

Instead one should do manually transform like this:

```
if random.random()<0.5:
  img = img_library.hflip(img)
  heatmap = img_library.hflip(img)
  landmark_point.x = img.width - landmark_point.x
```

It would be great if stochastic transform functions accept a list of images and coordinates and apply the same transform.

```
(img, heatmap), landmark_point = transforms.RandomHorizontalFlip(images=[img, heatmap], points=landmark_point)
```

issue_title: run_cqlsh_process always fails because of Python 3 string/bytes issues
issue_body:
On the cassandra-test branch, in ccmlib/node.py::Node, run_cqlsh_process splits and concatenates its cmds parameter with string arguments (which will fail if cmds was a bytes object), and writes it to a subprocess pipe to stdin (which will fail if cmds was a string object).

I propose to address this issue by adding a check near the beginning of run_cqlsh_process to encode cmds in utf-8 if it's not a bytes object, and by changing the string objects used for splitting and concatenation to bytes objects.

I'll submit a pull request to address this issue.

Luigi doesn't check default arguments when deciding whether a job is already running
issue_body:
I have a job with a `date` CLI argument that defaults to yesterday. It's cronned to run daily.

However, if there's a problem with the job and it doesn't terminate for one day, then the next day when the command is run, luigi reports that the job is already running, when in fact it isn't because the `date` parameter will now be different.

E.g.:

The job is invoked on 2015-03-30 with `my-job --another-arg=blah`. The date parameter is not explicitly passed so defaults to the day before, 2015-03-29. This job fails and retry logic keeps it around.
The next night, 2015-03-31, cron executes `my-job --another-arg=blah` again, and this time the `date` parameter defaults to the value 2015-03-30. Luigi should run this job but it doesn't. Instead it reports that the job is already running and exits.

issue_title: Batch notifier can stall the scheduler
issue_body:
So shortly after getting the new batch notifier merged, my connection to my smtp server died. I had timeout set to 30 seconds and 25 batch e-mails getting sent every hour, leading to the scheduler being stuck timing out e-mail sends for 12.5 minutes each hour.

I tried a patch that would fork and do the sending in the child process to avoid this failure mode, but tornado flipped out when that happened. Any suggestions on how we could do this more safely? Maybe we should use the multithreading or multiprocessing libraries to get something safer?

issue_title: Header Validation not being performed as expected
issue_body:
### Description

OpenAPI/Swagger spec defines a set of header(s) in its response block, like such:

    responses:
        '200':
          description: My Description.
          schema:
            type: array
            items:
              $ref: '#/definitions/MyObject'
          headers:
            Link:
              type: string

Appending the header is relatively easy using Connexion:

    return 'Foo', 200, {'Link': 'FOO'}

Which is returned correctly, however when the header is not added, Connexion is not marking it as a warning or throwing any errors for not matching the spec.

### Expected behavior

5XX response or something similar to indicate an internal error due to the API not satisfying the spec.

### Actual behavior

200 OK status code, response is returned anyway without the defined header.

### Additional Info

I checked in the repo itself, and can confirm that there is a code block dedicated to validating headers to make sure they match the spec:

`connexion.decorators.response.py`


    if response_definition and response_definition.get("headers"):
        # converting to set is needed to support python 2.7
        response_definition_header_keys = set(response_definition.get("headers").keys())
        header_keys = set(headers.keys())
        missing_keys = response_definition_header_keys - header_keys
        if missing_keys:
            pretty_list = ', '.join(missing_keys)
            msg = ("Keys in header don't match response specification. "
                   "Difference: {0}").format(pretty_list)
            raise NonConformingResponseHeaders(message=msg)


May I be missing something in particular in this case? Maybe not related to Connexion? 

PS - Running latest version of Connexion

issue_title: Separate unit tests from integration tests
issue_body:
Currently, there are many tests in the `test` directory that call external resources, rather than mocking requests/responses. Some also require environment variables (e.g. setting `EIA_KEY` in `test_eia.py`). This is causing issues in Travis and we're losing the value from existing continuous integration work that has been done.

I propose the following changes:
1. The codebase seems to follow a _[tests outside application code](https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/goodpractices.html)_ directory structure, which is fine. In line with [this Stack Overflow thread](https://docs.pytest.org/en/latest/goodpractices.html) I'd further organize directories into:
```
pyiso
  + --- tests
  |       + --- unit
  |       |         | test_base.py
  |       |         | test_client_one.py
  |       |         | test_client_two.py
  |       |
  |       + --- integration 
  |                 | integration_test_client_one.py
  |                 | integration_test_client_two.py
  |       
  + --- pyiso
          | base.py
          | client_one.py
          | client_two.py
```
2. Fix `travis.yml` so that the build server only runs unit tests when pull requests are opened. I believe this is just changing the `[nosetests](https://nose.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html)` script on line 26 to something like:
```
script: nosetests -w ./tests/unit
```

This only leaves one question: What automated process should run the integration tests? Should the maintainers just be responsible for running integration tests before formalizing a release? Should the integration tests just be there for developers to run out-of-band so they can be confident in their code before opening pull requests?

issue_title: fp - Weird float behavior around conversions?
issue_body:
I've the following benchmark:

```
(set-option :produce-models true)
(set-logic QF_FP)

; A free variable x
(declare-fun x () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53))

; Let y be the ulp
(define-fun  y () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53) (fp #b0 #b00000000000 #x0000000000001))

; Assert that x and y are floating-point equal.
(assert (fp.eq x y))

; Compute the conversion from double-to-float for each
(define-fun xf () (_ FloatingPoint 8 24) ((_ to_fp 8 24) roundNearestTiesToEven x))
(define-fun yf () (_ FloatingPoint 8 24) ((_ to_fp 8 24) roundNearestTiesToEven y))

; Results should be precisely the same float; so the following should be unsat
(assert (not (= xf yf)))

(check-sat)

(get-model)
```

It looks hairy; but it's essentially creating the smallest double value in `y`, and then asserts that `x` is floating-point equal to `y`. Which would only be satisfiable when `x` is also the smallest double. Then it goes ahead and casts them both to single-precision values; and asserts that the result should be the different. That query should be unsatisfiable; as both conversions should yield `+0`.

But when run, Z3 produces:

```
sat
(model
  (define-fun x () (_ FloatingPoint 11 53)
    (fp #b0 #b00000000000 #x0000000000001))
)
```

What's weird is that it says the model is satisfiable; but then suggests `x` must be the smallest double itself; i.e., in the model it created, the conversion to single-precision through `xf` and `yf` should be patently equal; thus making the query itself `unsat`.

issue_title: reduce_args tactic produces models with a default value only
issue_body:
The reduce_args tactic's model converter isn't playing very nicely. See the following example:

```Python
from z3 import *
f = Function('f', IntSort(), IntSort())

s = AndThen(Tactic('reduce-args'), Tactic('smt')).solver()
s.add(Or(f(1) == 2, f(2) == 3))
s.check()
print s.model()

s = Tactic('smt').solver()
s.add(Or(f(1) == 2, f(2) == 3))
s.check()
print s.model()
```

Outputs:
```
[f = [else -> If(Var(0) == 2, 0, 2)]]
[f = [1 -> 2, 2 -> 0, else -> 2]]
```

It would be nicer to get the map as we do without using the tactic, since then the API works properly (rather than potentially having to parse the "default" expression).

issue_title: Z3_parse_smtlib2_string function simplifies and rules out some variables to literals
issue_body:
Hi,
I'm using CBMC (CPROVER tools) to translate some (floating point variables involved) C programs to smtlib2 format and perform some analysis on them using Z3. I've got two set of variables, non-deterministic (input) variables and some random (output) variables. I need to count atomic predicates which random outputs participate in and then forge a closure of non-deterministic variables having an effect on those predicates.
The problem is, when i use "Z3_parse_smtlib2_string" function to parse the CBMC output and setup the basis formula in a z3 context and solver, the parser righteously simplifies and rules out some of my random outputs to bare literals. in some cases, it's extreme enough that the final formula lacks neither any kind of random output nor atomic predicates in which random outputs could participate.
I'm completely a newbie to Z3, and it's C/C++ API. I wanted to ask if there is a workaround to disable simplification of variables to literals while parsing. I would also appreciate any other idea than writing my own parser for directing analyzing smtlib2 file by CBMC.
Thanks in advance.

issue_title: Type-checking problems in n-ary equality
issue_body:
I observed the following behaviour in Z3 4.4.0 (x64 build on Win 7 x64). Let's assume the preamble

```
(set-option :type_check true)

(declare-const b (Bool))
(declare-const x (Int))
(declare-const y (Int))
```
As expected, `(assert (= x b))` yields a `sort mismatch` error.

Unexpectedly, `(assert (= x b y))` yields no error, and a subsequent `check-sat` even yields `sat`.

Moreover, the sequence
```
(assert (= x y z))
(check-sat)
(assert (= x b y))
(check-sat)
```
makes Z3 crash (`z3.exe has stopped working`).

issue_title: Access user object from AbstractPathIO
issue_body:
I'm trying to implement my own specialised FTP server by extending the `AbstractUserManager` and `AbstractPathIO`. However I have a requirement for the `AbstractPathIO` based class to know which user is accessing it. 

I'm basically passing the provided username and password onto a third-party service and need to be able to access them within every `AbstractPathIO` operation.

I would be tempted to make the `connection` object an arg on every `AbstractPathIO` method and pass it through on every call. However this is a large API change, and perhaps someone can think of a better way.

issue_title: list() method fails with KeyError when MLSD command returns no facts
issue_body:
I am trying to use aioftp as a client for an FTP server with an MLSD command that returns less information than LIST.  Specfically, it returns no facts, which is technically compliant with [RFC 3659](https://tools.ietf.org/html/rfc3659#section-7.2).   This causes the `list()` method to raise a KeyError, as it assumes the response will contain a fact with at least the dir property:
``` 
File "/Users/tkaplan/.local/share/virtualenvs/ad-pipes-Ecr7n0Zd/lib/python3.7/site-packages/aioftp/client.py", line 672, in __anext__
    if info["type"] == "dir" and recursive:
KeyError: 'type'
```
The fallback to the LIST method is not used in this case, since technically the server responds to the MLSD request.  A nice workaround would be to allow the user to choose whether to use the MLSD or LIST command.

issue_title: Make LineTooLong exception more detailed about actual data size.
issue_body:
## Long story short

Currently seeing such exceptions in logs doesn't help much in understanding how big the actual data was:
```python

aiohttp.http_exceptions.LineTooLong: 400, message='Got more than 8190 bytes when reading Header value is too long.'

Although `HttpParser` has the `max_field_size` argument allowing to control this limit it is better

So, I'd like to modify `LineTooLong` exception extending it with one more argument `actual_size`.

@aio-libs/aiohttp-committers, thoughts?

issue_title: Switch to using the thrift_sasl PyPI module
CONAN_DOCKER_IMAGE_SKIP_UPDATE doesn't work with conan docker tools
issue_body:
None of the images in conan_docker_tools install `conan_package_tools` by default.  So, if one uses `CONAN_DOCKER_IMAGE_SKIP_UPDATE` or the constructor parameter of the same name, builds will fail because there will be no script named `run_create_in_docker` in the container.  

Let me know if i've missed something.  If not, I can understand why one would skip installing `conan_package_tools` in the container, but since you do install `conan` in the container... it makes less sense.

issue_title: Don't let a single slow peer kill performance of chain sync
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

When doing a chain sync, for each chain segment we split the missing block parts (bodies, receipts) in N batches (where N is the number of connected peers that have those block parts) and request each batch to a different peer. Quite often the peers will not reply with all requested items (because they implement a byte-size cap in responses), so we need to wait for replies from all peers in order to figure out what block parts we're still missing so that we can make another round of requests.

This obviously doesn't perform well if we have a slow peer.

### How can it be fixed

Need to keep track of the avg response time (or throughput?) for peers and request body parts only to the ones we deem fast enough (e.g. top 2/3rds ranked by response time, or below a certain response time threshold)

issue_title: Web3.isAddress doesn't work for non prefixed checksumed values
issue_body:
* Version: 4.0.0b11
* Python: 3.6
* OS: linux

### What was wrong?

As stated in the docs http://web3py.readthedocs.io/en/latest/overview.html#Web3.isAddress the function Web3.isAddress(value) should **allow both 0x prefixed and non prefixed values**.

If the address is not checksumed, it's ok not to have the **0x**:

```
>>> Web3.isAddress('d3cda913deb6f67967b99d67acdfa1712c293601')
>>> True
```

But if it's checksumed

```
>>> Web3.isAddress('d3CdA913deB6f67967B99D67aCDFa1712C293601')
>>> False
```

No problem if we add the **0x**:

```
>>> Web3.isAddress('0xd3CdA913deB6f67967B99D67aCDFa1712C293601')
>>> True
```

### How can it be fixed?

Changing the documentation to state that checksumed addresses must have 0x or changing the function to accept checksumed addresses with 0x. I would just remove 0x at the beginning of the function (if found) and work with the address as that.

issue_title: Timeout exceeded for HTTPProvider
issue_body:
* Version: 4.4.0
* Python: 3.6
* OS: linux

### What was wrong?
Timeout exceeded when using `eth.getLogs` with HTTPProvider. I guess it can be reproduced too with any query to the Ethereum node that last longer than 10 seconds.

Problem is in `kwargs.setdefault('timeout', 10)` for the HTTPProvider

```python
def make_post_request(endpoint_uri, data, *args, **kwargs):
    kwargs.setdefault('timeout', 10)
    session = _get_session(endpoint_uri)
    response = session.post(endpoint_uri, data=data, *args, **kwargs)
    response.raise_for_status()

    return response.content
```

### How can it be fixed?

Make the parameter configurable or accept a kwargs param in eth calls to send a custom timeout

issue_title: Only try to resolve ENS names on mainnet (in the default middleware)
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

The ENS middleware will try to resolve names no matter what your network ID. Test chains shouldn't try to resolve these names, and give confusing errors when you accidentally supply a name-like value to an address field.

### How can it be fixed?

- Modify the ens middleware to initialize with a network ID and only attempt to resolve names if connected to that network ID.
- When validating whether a value is acceptable input for an address, only accept an ENS name if connected to the correct network ID.
- Set up the default middleware to use network ID 1 (for mainnet)

issue_title: Stop encoding keccak arguments with latin-1
issue_body:
### What was wrong?

`eth_utils.keccak()` is encoding text arguments with `latin-1`. This is different from behavior of other eth libraries.

This is a sub-task of #36 

See https://gist.github.com/holiman/6f57cb5b082b6712b9e8c19fe48ca960#gistcomment-2332077

### How can it be fixed?

One of:

- only accept bytes as argument
- convert text to utf-8
- **Edit:** use the `(primitive=None, texstr=None, hexstr=None)` argument signature approach -- *preferred*

Probably requires a major version bump

issue_title: Add global clock
issue_body:
Watchman does not currently "support symlinks".
https://github.com/facebook/watchman/issues/105#issuecomment-206463325 has some thoughts on how we might address this.

This particular issue encompasses one portion of this effort:

# Global Clock

Right now we track an abstract clock per watched project. There are two components to the clock; the root number and the tick counter. Each time a change is observed, the tick counter is incremented. This is fundamentally how we perform time-based queries. The root number is bumped each time we recrawl a root and allows us to disambiguate certain classes of stale clock values when deciding which events a client has missed.

Since each watch ticks independently, and symlinks can point to many different projects, there are multiple different clocks. We'd need some basis for synchronizing these watches to be able to meaningfully and easily query across them.

One possibility is adding a global tick counter that is bumped whenever a change is observed. We'd then add a global clock file to the state that we track per file, and update this at the same time that we update the existing `oclock` field.  We'd then need to add a `global_since` generator to the query engine to indicate that this is the query strategy that should be used.

# What to do in code?

* Add a global atomics counters: `global_ticks`.
* Add a `uint32_t global_tick` to `watchman_file`.  Whenever we `mark_file_changed`, we'd sample the `global_ticks` value and store it in the `file->global_tick` field
* Add a `global_since` generator to the query syntax.  The implementation of this would be basically the same as `time_generator` in `query/eval.c` but it would compare the `global_ticks` portion of the clock.
* Add a `global_clock` field to the query response object and to the result of the `clock` command so that clients can be aware of this global clock value
* Add a `global_since` query term as an alternative to https://facebook.github.io/watchman/docs/expr/since.html

issue_title: Helpers for writing series
issue_body:
I'll be working on the following from [here](https://github.com/ChristopherRabotin/influxdb-python).

A class ought to help developers avoid mistakes when creating the JSON for the `write_points` method, while also enable automatic bulk writes.

Initial helper class to be used as follows (comments wanted).

```
# Definition
class Event(InfluxDBHelper):
    class Meta:
        # server_name to be set for each event on commit.
        series_name = "event.stats.{server_name}"
        # Explicit.
        fields = ["time", "server_name", "sequence_number", "line"]
        # Raise an exception if a defined field is missing or additional fields provided.
        enforce_fields = False
        # Defines how many items should be stacked before a write request.
        bulk_size = 50
        # Commit if object is constructed with all fields defined in `fields`.
        auto_commit = False 

# Usage
# -- The three following are equivalent.
event = Event(**{"time": 1400425947368, "server_name": "us.east-1", "line": "some string"})
# or
event = Event(time=1400425947368, server_name="us.east-1", line="some string")
# or
event = Event()
event.time = 1400425947368
event.server_name = "us.east-1"
event.line = "some string"

# Set record in memory and will be committed when the bulk size is reached.
event.commit()

# Force commit of all items waiting for bulk size.
Event.commit_all() # Note that we're calling the class.
```

issue_title: from_DSN won't work with upper case passwords
issue_body:
Hello,

If the password given in the dsn string to InfluxDBClient.from_DSN contains some upper case letters, then subsequent queries on the client will fail with:
```
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/influxdb/influxdb08/client.py", line 419, in query
    chunked=chunked)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/influxdb/influxdb08/client.py", line 447, in _query
    expected_response_code=200
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/influxdb/influxdb08/client.py", line 255, in request
    raise InfluxDBClientError(response.content, response.status_code)
influxdb.influxdb08.client.InfluxDBClientError: 401: Invalid username/password
```

Good practice for passwords is to mix lower and upper cases.
I guess other params like username and database name in the dsn could also have this problem if they are case sensitive as well. I did not test them.

For the moment it can be solved by giving the password as an extra parameter to from_DSN. But it could confuse other users and take them a while to understand.


Cheers,
Axel

issue_title: Upgrade to Python 3: migrations broken
issue_body:
### Problem
Upgrading a Django project to Python 3 and Django 2: ./manage.py migrate falls over a bytestring:
Note I've hand-inserted a print() statement into $VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/db/models/manager.py
```
Model <class '__fake__.Site'>, name b'objects' # print from django.db.models.manager

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "./manage.py", line 12, in <module>
    execute_from_command_line(sys.argv)
  File "$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/core/management/__init__.py", line 371, in execute_from_command_line
    utility.execute()
  File "$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/core/management/__init__.py", line 365, in execute
    self.fetch_command(subcommand).run_from_argv(self.argv)
  File "$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/core/management/base.py", line 288, in run_from_argv
    self.execute(*args, **cmd_options)
  File "$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/core/management/base.py", line 335, in execute
    output = self.handle(*args, **options)
  File "$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/core/management/commands/migrate.py", line 160, in handle
    pre_migrate_apps = pre_migrate_state.apps
  File "$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/utils/functional.py", line 36, in __get__
    res = instance.__dict__[self.name] = self.func(instance)
  File "$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/db/migrations/state.py", line 210, in apps
    return StateApps(self.real_apps, self.models)
  File "$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/db/migrations/state.py", line 271, in __init__
    self.render_multiple(list(models.values()) + self.real_models)
  File "$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/db/migrations/state.py", line 306, in render_multiple
    model.render(self)
  File "$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/db/migrations/state.py", line 575, in render
    return type(self.name, bases, body)
  File "$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/db/models/base.py", line 152, in __new__
    new_class.add_to_class(obj_name, obj)
  File "$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/db/models/base.py", line 315, in add_to_class
    value.contribute_to_class(cls, name)
  File "$VIRTUAL_ENV/lib/python3.6/site-packages/django/db/models/manager.py", line 117, in contribute_to_class
    setattr(model, name, ManagerDescriptor(self))
TypeError: attribute name must be string, not 'bytes'
```

### Cause
There is a stray bytestring (`b''`) in migration 0003. 
All migrations are parsed, even if they already have been applied, so Django will trip over any bytestring.
Since unicode_literals are imported (and the string in question is empty anyways), it should be safe to drop the `b` prefix.

issue_title: Use ManifestStaticFilesStorage instead of CachedStaticFilesStorage
issue_body:
Question : Would ManifestStaticFilesStorage be a suitable replacement when using cache busting url for this project ? 

Reason : According to recent Django [docs](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.11/ref/contrib/staticfiles/#django.contrib.staticfiles.storage.CachedStaticFilesStorage)

> *Warning*
> CachedStaticFilesStorage isn’t recommended – in almost all cases ManifestStaticFilesStorage is a better choice. There are several performance penalties when using CachedStaticFilesStorage since a cache miss requires hashing files at runtime. Remote file storage require several round-trips to hash a file on a cache miss, as several file accesses are required to ensure that the file hash is correct in the case of nested file paths.

Another good article : https://devblog.kogan.com/blog/a-hidden-gem-in-django-1-7-manifeststaticfilesstorage

1. Sync from Google to local
2. Sync from local to Mailfence
3. Repair unsafe UID on Mailfence
4. Wipe out local (store and status)
5. Sync Mailfence to local
6. Sync local to Mailbox (with manual throttling through KeyboardInterrupt)

### Expected behavior
- Items are uploaded to Mailbox until keyboard interrupt (Ctrl-C).
- Then, when sync is resumed (rerun), some more items are uploaded.

issue_title: Serial timeouts are the wrong interval when system clock is set.
issue_body:
I have been having some really hard to find bugs with serial communication locking up when the system clock gets updated.I tracked it back to the use of time.time() instead of time.monotonic() in serialposix.py.
The problem is that timeouts are calculated by comparing a previous call to time.time() to a current one. This is a big problem if the system clock is changed backwards. This effectively adds (or subtracts) the changed system time to the timeout. This is what time.monotonic() is intended to avoid.

From a distribution (as Debian) point of view, this is a far more pertinent approach: one debball for the cysignals core, and debballs for the modules/plugins (PARI and alikes). From an upstream point of view, it enlarges potential usages.

issue_title: Wheel requirements output specifiers in requirement lines
Mark run ids archived to Glacier as processed
issue_body:
One problem here is that it doesn't seems possible to check if some prefix like `enriched-archive/run=2017-04-27-14-39-42/` was archived to glacier by simply listing `enriched-archive` and checking metadata. It's possible to archive *object*, not *prefix*.

Therefore, we most likely need to:
1. List prefixes in enriched archive
2. For each of prefix get `part-00001` key
3. Check if this key has storage class `GLACIER`
4. If it does - mark as processed, if doesn't - unprocessed, if key doesn't exist - unprocessed (?)

Approach seems a bit fragile because for some keys in prefix can probably be archived and some can be not. Not sure how likely.

Another approach would be to get lifecycle policy for bucket. And based on prefix, current date, and date in run id decide if this prefix is archived. This approach is fragile because it's not possible restore and reprocess run id.

@alexanderdean few more questions:
1. Should we just not return archived run ids from `list_run_ids` or add them to DynamoDB as well?
2. What release version it should be? 0.2.2 or 0.3.0?

While testing the sync functionality, I decided to turn my VPN off mid sync. After a good while (at least 10min?) uploads managed to continue. A while later, I turned on VPN again, and uploads again stopped for quite a while. However, a while later I got two errors:

```
b2_upload(/mnt/data/borgtest/data/0/249, data/0/249, 1470290017627): UnknownError() unknown error: ReadTimeout(ReadTimeoutError("HTTPSConnectionPool(host='pod-000-1005-09.backblaze.com', port=443): Read timed out. (read timeout=None)",),)

Those two files may have been uploading when I turned VPN on, but I imagine others would have been as well.

Running `b2 sync` again it happily uploaded the remaining two(!) files.

TL:DR

- Sync remained open, waiting (forever?) for two failed uploads to complete.

issue_title: Change in behaviour of neuron checking functions
issue_body:
The neuron checking functions were designed at a time when NeuroM was an SWC-only package. As such, it made sense to return IDs of points that caused failures. This was intended to help determined users to debug SWC morphology files. However, NeuroM now deals with HDF5 and (experimentally) with NeuriLucida .asc files, for which the concept of point ID is meaningless. 

I suggest changing the reporting of failures to either return nothing, or return a section ID, point ID pair. The latter can be used to debug interactively and programatically (but should not be persisted.)

Does this make sense? And if so, do we return nothing, or the ID pair?

issue_title: Making cloudpickle produce "consistent/deterministic" results.
issue_body:
This question arose in the following context. I have multiple Python processes, and some classes are defined in each process. Sometimes class definitions are shipped from one process to another (using cloudpickle). Sometimes classes may be shipped multiple times or in multiple ways to a given process, and I'd like to deduplicate them based on the output of cloudpickle (that is, if `cloudpickle.dumps(class1) == cloudpickle.dumps(class2)`, then the classes are the "same" and I can throw away one of them. This works, but there are way too many false negatives (that is, two classes really are the same (in some sense), but `cloudpickle.dumps` gives different results on the two classes.

Here's one example that sort of illustrates the issue (although there are a number of ways this kind of thing can arise).

Suppose I do the following.

```python
import cloudpickle

class Foo1(object):
    def __init__(self):
        pass

serialized1 = cloudpickle.dumps(Foo1)
Foo2 = cloudpickle.loads(serialized1)
serialized2 = cloudpickle.dumps(Foo2)

assert serialized1 == serialized2  # This assertion fails.
```

I'd love for this kind of assertion to succeed. **Does anyone know if this is achievable or what the main obstacles are?**

Interestingly, if I iterate this a third time,

```python
Foo3 = cloudpickle.loads(serialized2)
serialized3 = cloudpickle.dumps(Foo3)

assert serialized2 == serialized3  # This succeeds.
```

then the assert succeeds, so maybe it suffices to use `cloudpickle.dumps(cloudpickle.loads(cloudpickle.dumps(cls)))` to deduplicate classes (though this seems kind of insane, and I haven't tested this extensively). **Would you expect this to work?**

One thing that may be related/revealing is the outputs I get if I do something similar in an IPython interpreter (instead of a regular Python interpreter). 

First copy and paste this block into IPython.

```python
import cloudpickle

class Foo(object):
    def __init__(self):
        pass

serialized1 = cloudpickle.dumps(Foo)
```

Then copy and paste this block into IPython.

```python
class Foo(object):
    def __init__(self):
        pass

serialized2 = cloudpickle.dumps(Foo)
```

Comparing `serialized1` and `serialized2` next to each other, they are

```

issue_title: Multiple responses not working in 0.6.1
issue_body:
In 0.6.1 it seems that adding multiple responses no longer works.

Using the multiple responses example from the README:
```
$ cat test.py
import responses
import requests

with responses.RequestsMock(assert_all_requests_are_fired=True) as rsps:
    rsps.add(responses.GET, 'http://twitter.com/api/1/foobar', status=500)
    rsps.add(responses.GET, 'http://twitter.com/api/1/foobar',
    	 body='{}', status=200,
    	 content_type='application/json')

    resp = requests.get('http://twitter.com/api/1/foobar')
    assert resp.status_code == 500
    resp = requests.get('http://twitter.com/api/1/foobar')
    assert resp.status_code == 200
$ pip install responses==0.6.0
...
$ python test.py
$ pip install responses==0.6.1
...
$ python test.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 14, in <module>
    assert resp.status_code == 200
AssertionError
```

Seems to keep returning the first response.

This is on Ubuntu 16.04, Python 2.7.12

issue_title: Assertion Errors

Love the library. It is super useful! 

I am trying to figure out how to see if a function raises an exception when called with a certain set of parameters. 

Here is an example (a bit contrived as I am trying to raise my own Exception based on valid inputs). 

```python
t = [0,1,2]
t.index(3) | should.raise_error(ValueError)

t.index | should.raise_error(ValueError)

```python
err = ""
try:
    self.index(3)
except Exception as err:
   error = err

error | should.be.an.instance.of(ValueError)
```

Am I missing Something?

issue_title: Split Exception types: processing schema and runtime
issue_body:
Right now we have one python file where error granularity is defined.

We should make explicit a separation:

* Exceptions raised while processing the schema (such as when a window dtc refers to a non-existing datagroup in a streaming schema)
* Exceptions raised while executing the transformation (runtime)

The reason is that both errors are handled differently:

The 1st type of error is the user's fault. Something's wrong with the input, and there should be an explicit, properly formatted, tested and reviewed error message to be displayed.

The 2nd is an internal error. If there's no errors of the 1st type, 2nd errors shouldn't be produced. A full stack trace should be reported in this case.

issue_title: handling level=0 in waverec/wavedec functions
issue_body:
I found an inconsistency in all ``wavedec`` functions relative to their ``waverec`` counterparts.  If the signal is too small for the chosen wavelet, ``level`` will be set to 0 in ``wavedec`` and a length 1 ``coeffs_list`` containing only the original data will be returned.  If this is then passed in to ``waverec``, an error will occur, because it only allows coefficient lists of length >= 2.

There are two potential solutions here:
1.) raise an error in ``_check_level`` if level = 0.
2.) modify ``waverecn`` to allow length 1 coefficient arrays and just return coeffs[0] (which is the original data) if the coeffs input was length 1. 

I would lean toward solution 2, possibly adding a warning in ``wavedecn`` and ``waverecn`` if level=0.  My reasoning is that if we allow the ``wavedecn`` operation at level=0 we should also allow a proper roundtrip back via ``wavedecn``.

issue_title: Return Date fields as datetime instances, not strings
issue_body:
It would be very convenient to have LastUpdateDate, AcceptedDate, etc. returned as timezone-aware datetime instances. That's what they really are, anyway. Other attributes such as Defect.Project or Defect.Release are already returned as the appropriate entity type, so returning datetimes here would improve consistency in the API. It would also provide the user immediate access to useful datetime methods instead of having to parse the string.

EntityHydrator._setAppropriateAttrValueForType() before:

        if type(attrValue) != dict:
            setattr(instance, attrName, attrValue)
            return

After:

        if type(attrValue) != dict:
            if attrName.endswith('Date') and attrValue:
                # Python doesn't recognize 'Z' as a valid timezone offset, so replace it with '+0000'.
                attrValue = datetime.datetime.strptime(attrValue[:-1] + '+0000', '%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%f%z')
            setattr(instance, attrName, attrValue)
            return

This happens because by default normalize is True and will create the self._norm.
https://github.com/biocommons/hgvs/blob/82c898a11dea70e671415a3d9dcc79b03005051e/hgvs/assemblymapper.py#L70-L71

But in `_maybe_normalize`, it checks `self._norm` rather than `self.normalize`. Thus, even after self.normalize becomes False, self._norm is still not None and projected variants would still be normalized.

https://github.com/biocommons/hgvs/blob/82c898a11dea70e671415a3d9dcc79b03005051e/hgvs/assemblymapper.py#L182-L189

issue_title: AWS::CertificateManager::Certificate is missing
incompatible changes in 3.9.1
issue_body:
With 70a7b5a16748afbf0d48ca180c2b7613fdd7e7d0 we introduced some  backwards incompatible changes:

 * `Component.is_broken` got replaced with `Component.errors`
 * events with a `RDATE;VALUE=PERIOD:19970101T180000Z/19970102T070000Z,19970109T180000Z/PT5H30M` component still had an `RDATE` with `VALUE=PERIOD` param before, now they are `RDATE:None`

While I do agree with both changes, I think they should have been deferred to the 4.0.0 release. Because we don't have `VALUE=PEORIOD` anyway I think we can leave this one as it is, but I believe we should bring back `Component.is_broken` for the 3.9.3 release.

issue_title: API naming variable changes
issue_body:
@garrettjonesgoogle @bjwatson @omaray 

### Background

Some recent changes have introduced inconsistencies in how we refer to the gRPC Python packages -- just wanted to get input on how we should resolve this. Renaming the packages at a future date is a breaking change, and it's linked to some changes I want to make around how we treat the concept of "API names" in our tooling in connection to googleapis/toolkit#270, so I wanted to get some feedback before I implement this.

### Currently

- Artman currently has a single "API name" concept, which is configurable in the artman config.
- A sample API name might be google-cloud-logging-v2
- Our packaging tools (Packman, and its successor MetadataGen) further introduce two more API name variables:
    - The "version" is the last word in the API name, e.g., "v2"
    - The "short name" is the second-to-last word in the API name, e.g., "logging"
    - The "title name" is `Google {titlecased short name}`, e.g., "Google Logging". This is used in the autogenerated README to refer to the service.
- The gRPC Python package name is either `grpc-google-{shortname}-{version}` or `grpc-{api name}`, depending on where you look (i.e., either `grpc-google-logging-v2` or `grpc-google-cloud-logging-v2`). That's a problem.

### Proposal

- I want to get rid of the "API name" configuration value in artman. The existing variables will be replaced as follows:
    - Version --> independent configuration value in artman config
    - Short name --> independent configuration value in artman config
    - Title name --> use `title` attribute from service config (example)
    - API Name --> `google-cloud-{short name}-{version}`
- The Python package names will then be `grpc-{API Name}` and `gapic-{API Name}`.

issue_title: Lambda python handler path cannot use dot notation on deeply-nested modules
issue_body:
AWS Lambda allows (at least a python) handler in a deeply-nested modules to be specified with dotted notation. E.g.,`foo.bar.baz.handler`, where `handler` is a method in `foo/bar/baz.py`.

However localstack fails on `awslocal lambda create-function` with "Unable to find handler script in Lambda archive." unless the handler is in a top-level module (e.g., `foo.handler`, where `handler` is a method in `foo.py`.

As a workaround,`foo/bar/baz.handler` works on both localstack and AWS. But this seems inelegant, as that notation isn't meaningful anywhere else in pythonland.

Suggestion: 

The `split` stanza at the following line:

https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/master/localstack/services/awslambda/lambda_api.py#L392

Could be changed to something like:

`handler_name.rpartition('.')[0].replace('.', '/')`

although I'm not sure if that logic is apropos for JavaScript or not.

issue_title: Event listeners (and filtering) for state events
issue_body:
Following conversation on #43:

> Happy with #44 for keeping old functionality.
> 
> Now, onto the other issues:
> > there are so many different ways to divide the events up into groups that one may want to listen to. For example, currently per-room and per-client listeners are supported (modulo this bug report); the docstring ("Incoming event callbacks") seems to also indicate per-user listeners.
> 
> Regrettably, we do not have per user listeners. Users are 'fun' because we can either have profile wide changes or room wide changes to them (via m.room.member events which usually but not always are global changes, so context is everything!) . 
> 
> > I find that behaviour pretty simple and intuitive, but we could be more granular adding arguments to the two add_listener functions (like event_type='m.room.message' or event_family='state')
> 
> I like this. It might be the best way to do it because I think specific *functions* for each type is ridiculous. Potentially you might want to add a few wrappers for things like topic, but I'm not fussed. Event family is probably overkill, ``add_state_listener`` and ``add_listener`` would be quite fine. I also would add an option for exclusivity if people want it.
> 
> You'd end up with something like:
> ``add_state_listener(func, event_type=None, exclusive=False)``
>

issue_title: test: "fuzzing" OpenThread to provide robustness and negative stress testing
issue_body:
We have good positive path test coverage of OpenThread now using `make check` and the CI test suite.

Perhaps the negative path test coverage of OpenThread could be built using "fuzzing" techniques, and some of the tools available in that space.

The GDS [blog](http://blog.gdssecurity.com/labs/2015/9/21/fuzzing-the-mbed-tls-library.html) discusses the technique, good open source tools to implement it, and even have examples applying the method to an instrumented mbedtls:

> "Fuzzing is a testing technique that feeds a program under inspection with random inputs and observes its behavior. The goal of fuzzing is to discover bugs in the inspected program."

issue_title: Spinel: Separate Definition of Protocol Basis
issue_body: Presentation of the Spinel protocol to IETF would likely find an easier path to publication as Request For Comment (RFC) if the general-purpose basis of the Spinel protocol were documented separately from the specialization of the Spinel protocol for Thread™ network control processors.

issue_title: nRF52840: Pass up link quality information of the ACK frames
issue_body: #1836 passed up the ACK Psdu, while the link quality information of the ACK frame is not passed up. Another enhancement in #1790 is that all ACK messages will be used for link quality evaluation, so it would be better to also pass up the link quality information of the ACK frames.

issue_title: pex swallows SystemExit output
issue_body:
docopt is a command-line parsing library. If a user presents an invalid command-line, docopt raises  SystemExit with help output. (https://github.com/docopt/docopt/blob/master/docopt.py#L22)

Since https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/276/diff/1#index_header, pex swallows this output, leading to no output on the command-line. This severely restricts the usefulness of docopt when used with pex files. 

$ cat sysexit.py 
raise SystemExit('whoops')

$ python sysexit.py 
whoops # exit 1

Current pex:
$ pex -o sysexit.pex -m sysexit  ; ./sysexit.pex
<nothing>  # exit 1

I rolled a patch to catch BaseException & print just the exception type & message, dropping the traceback. This results in:

Patched pex:
$ pex -o sysexit.pex -m sysexit  ; ./sysexit.pex
SystemExit: whoops  # exit 1

This seems superior, if still not ideal, as it isn't directly equivalent to running the module with raw python. Other options may be to rebind or modify sys.excepthook or to avoid calling it.

issue_title: The Cobertura plugin includes a slf4j implementation on the classpath
issue_body:
The Pants Cobertura plugin includes `slf4j-simple` into the runtime classpath; this causes some of our tests to fail due to slf4j binding to the wrong implementation.

Removing the implementation from the Cobertura runtime classpath resolves this issue for me; I've also removed our slf4j implementation from the classpath and the coverage still runs without error.
```
    register_jvm_tool(register,
                      'cobertura-run',
                      classpath=[
                        cobertura_jar(intransitive=True),
                        #slf4j_jar
                      ])
```
I've also been able to remove it from the instrumentation runtime and I've not seen any errors (although leaving it in place doesn't cause a problem for us).
```
    register_jvm_tool(register,
                      'cobertura-instrument',
                      classpath=[
                        cobertura_jar(),
                        #slf4j_jar
                      ])
```

seems like this is potentially related to out of band SSL configuration changes on S3?

issue_title: Add an Android README
issue_body:
Now that this is exercised by CI and safely isolated in contrib - it could use a dedicated README. The base is very solid - it is ready for anyone who wants to consider taking it the rest of the way to production. A README with the supported features and known issues/TODOs would be useful.

It also needs some dependency information - as raised in #3151, compiling and testing the Android code requires particular SDK modules to be installed. Those dependencies should be documented.

issue_title: Use target type when matching source roots.
issue_body:
E.g., `JavaLibrary.target_base` should only match source roots that were registered against the language `java`.  Today we match source roots without regard for language, under the assumption that the repo doesn't have nesting source roots for different languages.

This would allow repos to have their `.proto` file imports be relative to the buildroot (as Google recommends), without that root then being returned as the target_base for all targets in all languages.

An alternative solution would be to take the longest match in the source root trie, instead of the shortest one as we do today.

issue_title: The setup_repo docs describe setting up source_roots but neglect registering bootstrap BUILD files
use xargs in junit_run to bypass ARG_MAX
issue_body:
Background: 

We recently saw some targets failing on Linux with messages like "Exception message: Problem executing /usr/lib/jvm/java-1.7.0-openjdk/bin/java: [Errno 7] Argument list too long". Running "getconf ARG_MAX" returns 131072. Obviously this is a fat target, with ~2k dependencies in the classpath, so we are reaching limit.

As @jsirois commented in [pants-devl](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/pants-devel/mkvjf4uFLkY), instead of using nailgun, use xargs

Xargs is here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/master/src/python/pants/process/xargs.py#L12
A use is here: https://github.com/pantsbuild/pants/blob/cd975096645d9c655a3d405e465256a2077387a1/src/python/pants/backend/jvm/tasks/checkstyle.py#L107-L113

issue_title: Backup Endpoint Changed
search method should support start_index and max_results
issue_body:
# Issue
CROWD API search results that exceed the server's maximum result set size are not accessible through the python-crowd.py project. The current search method supports CROWD API query parameter of entity_type for the /usermanagement/1/search resource, but has not implemented support for start_index or max_results parameters. 

# Solution
I propose to enhance the search method to implement two additional query parameters which the CROWD API supports: start_index and max_results. Implementation should include default values of start_index=0 and max_results=99999 to maintain backwards compatibility with existing tools not currently supplying these arguments when calling the search method.

# Benefits
Supporting start_index and max_results query parameters will…

- Allow retrieval of results exceeding the CROWD server's maximum results set size.
- Allow performance tuning if multiple, smaller query results would be better for server or client performance.

# Potential Issues
Existing tools using the python-crowd module that rely upon a server's default max_results setting may encounter a change in behavior as the query results could be larger (or smaller) than anticipated due to the proposed default value of 99999. According to the Atlassian REST API policy, the default and maximum sizes of paged data are not considered part of the API and may change without notice. Tools using the CROWD API should declare and validate results set sizes themselves. [https://developer.atlassian.com/platform/marketplace/atlassian-rest-api-policy/#AtlassianRESTAPIpolicy-Resource/ratelimitsandpaging](https://developer.atlassian.com/platform/marketplace/atlassian-rest-api-policy/#AtlassianRESTAPIpolicy-Resource/ratelimitsandpaging)

issue_title: Valid package names are stored incorrectly with new version of bottle
issue_body:
Not sure when this was introduced, but pypiserver 1.1.2 didn't have this issue. From the [bottle API docs](http://bottlepy.org/docs/dev/api.html#bottle.FileUpload.filename):

> Name of the file on the client file system, but normalized to ensure file system compatibility. An empty filename is returned as ‘empty’. Only ASCII letters, digits, dashes, underscores and dots are allowed in the final filename. Accents are removed, if possible. Whitespace is replaced by a single dash. Leading or tailing dots or dashes are removed. The filename is limited to 255 characters.

For example, the version `0.0.0+local` is a valid package version, but bottle gets rid of the `+`. I'm sure there are other combinations that also break.

issue_title: Documentation doesn't match stable version
issue_body:
The documentation is build from master whenever there is a commit on master, which is nice for some things like errors or omissions in the docs, but not so good for new features. It is unclear which features the latest stable version supports. For example, the docs currently list bigo as a supported site, however it is only supported in the nightly/HEAD version rather than the latest tagged version (0.1.0). 

There are a couple of ways I can think of resolving this. 
1. We could have a separate repo for the docs and ask people to submit separate PRs to update the docs. This might lead to the docs going unloved as it's an extra bit of work.
2. We could build the docs only on tagged revisions. This would fix the new features only in nightlies, but we'd have to have a tag/release to fix errors and omissions in the docs. We can build multiple versions of the docs, for each tag and for the current HEAD revision (similar to the readthedocs site).
3. We could mark each feature with the version that it was added or if it is currently only supported in the nightly version. This would be more work to update the versions when we release a new version as we'd have to updated all the "this feature is only supported in the nightly version" type messages to "added in x.y.z". It's possible that some automation (sphinx magic) could be used to make the release managers life easier. 

All of these suggestions add extra work, but would ultimately would benefit the users. If anyone has any other suggestions to improve the quality of the docs they would be most welcome. 

I think the 3rd option is pretty good, but the 2nd option would be OK too. 

Thought? Suggestions?

issue_title: Creating visualizations combining multiple tags
issue_body:
Currently, as I understand it, there is a one-to-one correspondence between TensorBoard visualizations (plots etc.) and summary tags in the events file. This forces the user to modify what gets written to the events file to change what plots (or other visualizations) get produced, even when the data necessary to make the desired plot already exists in the events file.

The only mechanism TensorBoard has for grouping multiple data series onto the same axes is to make them different runs with the exact same tag. However, this will often require a user to maintain multiple SummaryFileWriters which is inconvenient and must be done anew for every piece of experiment code (things in TF like tf.train.MonitoredSession do not make this easy by default).

As a motivating example of the types of things I want to do, I want to make a training curve for a single run of a TensorFlow program that is already started running and is taking a very long time. I want to compare a scalar summary with tag train_loss and a scalar summary with tag:validataion_loss on the same axes with global_step on the abscissa. I would like to tell the UI somehow to merge the two plots it already creates. Another thing I want to do is to show these two tags across several runs of my program, but I want to have the color based on the run and the line style (or marker style) based on the tag. Both of these things are hard to do given that I have already started writing the events file.

Although I am most concerned about scalar summaries, this problem can arise with lots of other types of data, including image summaries. @dandelionmane might have a good example for image summaries where this type of configurable grouping would be useful.

Currently what I do is just abandon TensorBoard and write my own ipython notebook to read the events files and make the plots I want and I copy this for each new project. So this is a critical missing feature. Although it is possible to get part of the behavior I want by making multiple subfolders and using the same tag, I don't always know what curves I want to group among my scalar summaries when I am writing the code that writes the events files and even when I do this clutters up my training and eval code.

issue_title: SampleTauTestCase.test_ratio failing inconsistently
issue_body:
The SampleTauTestCase.test_ratio test is failing inconsistently.  The self.assertTrue
was set at the new value of 0.002 introduced at https://github.com/blue-yonder/tsfresh/commit/a57a09fe62a62fe0d2564a056f7fd99f58822312 and this fails between 14 and 24
times in 100 in my testing.

Testing this at the original 0.001 value pre the above commit results in failure
between 46 and 61 times in 100 in my testing.
Further testing at 0.003, 0.004 and 0.005 all generate a decreasing amount of
errors.

Using the value of 0.006 seems to generate 0 errors on repeated tests of 100
iterations.

This test can be easily tested and reproduced with the following code:

```python
import numpy as np
from tsfresh.examples.driftbif_simulation import sample_tau

less_than_value = 0.002
# less_than_value = 0.003
# less_than_value = 0.004
# less_than_value = 0.005
# less_than_value = 0.006

count = 0
errors = 0
for i in range(100):
    count += 1
    tau = sample_tau(100000, ratio=0.4)
    sample = np.array(tau)
    before = np.sum(sample <= 1/0.3)
    beyond = np.sum(sample > 1/0.3)
    abs_value = abs(0.4 - float(before)/(before+beyond))
    if abs_value < less_than_value:
        print('%s - before - %s, beyond - %s, abs_value - %s' % (str(count), str(before), str(beyond), str(abs_value)))
    else:
        print('ERROR :: %s - before - %s, beyond - %s, abs_value - %s' % (str(count), str(before), str(beyond), str(abs_value)))
        errors += 1

print('%s errors in %s tests' % (str(errors), str(count)))
```

@nils-braun any thoughts here, as it stands now this test is going to fail ~14-22% of the time it seems.

issue_title: binary / script flag
issue_body:
When using `qsub` with SGE, there is a flag `-b` that specifies whether to treat the command as a binary or script. if `-b y`, `qsub` will assume the command is available on the compute hosts (and not necessarily the submission host) whereas if `-b n`, the file will be copied over from the submission.

 Is there a way to do that in `drmaaa` or should I use either `nativeSpecification` or something like `transferFiles`?

Full text from man page:


       -b y[es]|n[o]
              Available for qsub, qrsh only. Qalter does not allow changing this option. This option cannot be embedded in the script file itself.

              Gives  the  user the possibility to indicate explicitly whether command should be treated as binary or script. If the argument of -b is ’y’, then command  may be a binary or script.  The command might
              not be accessible from the submission host.  Nothing except the path of the command will be transferred from the submission host to the execution host. Path aliasing will be applied  to  the  path  of
              command before it is executed.

              If  the  argument  of  -b is ’n’ then command needs to be a script, and it will be handled as such. The script file has to be accessible by the submission host. It will be transferred to the execution
              host. qsub/qrsh will search directive prefixes within scripts.

              qsub will implicitly use -b n, whereas qrsh will apply the -b y option if nothing else is specified.

              The value specified with this option or the corresponding value specified in qmon will only be passed to defined JSV instances if the value is yes.  The name of the parameter will be b. The value will
              be y also when the long form yes was specified during submission.  (See -jsv option below or find more information concerning JSV in jsv(1).)

              Please  note  that submission of command as a script (-b n) can have a significant performance impact, especially for short running jobs and big job scripts.  Script submission adds a number of opera-
              tions to the submission process: The job script needs to be
              - parsed at client side (for special comments)
              - transferred from submit client to qmaster
              - spooled in qmaster
              - transferred to execd at job execution
              - spooled in execd
              - removed from spooling both in execd and qmaster once the job is done
              If job scripts are available on the execution nodes, e.g. via NFS, binary submission can be the better choice.

issue_title: Actor placement and task scheduling are not aware of each other.
issue_body:
This is most relevant in the case of actors and tasks that both require GPUs (perhaps not the most common case).

Suppose that we have two machines each with 1 GPU. We create one actor that uses 1 GPU and one task that uses 1 GPU. It is possible that they will be placed on the same machine. In that case, if the actor is created first, the task will never get scheduled because the actor may be around forever.

```python
import ray
import time

# This example should be run on a two node cluster in which each node has a single GPU.
ray.init(redis_address=...)

@ray.actor(num_gpus=1)
class Foo(object):
  def __init__(self):
    pass

@ray.remote(num_gpus=1)
def f():
  return 1

# Create an actor. This uses 1 GPU.
Foo()

time.sleep(1)

# Wait for the GPU task to finish. This may hang.
ray.get(f.remote())
```

issue_title: Document when the new version of a remote function is used when the definition changes.
issue_body:
We should document precisely what happens when a remote function is redefined (this should go in the troubleshooting documentation). In particular, does the new version get run or the old version? There are some subtleties.

**Case 1: `f` redefined.**

For example, if we define a function and then redefine it, the new version should run.

```python
@ray.remote
def f():
  return 1

@ray.remote
def f():
  return 2

ray.get(f.remote())  # This should be 2.
```

**Case 2: `f` in a file and reimported.**

What if the definition of `f` is in a file `temp.py`? Suppose we `import temp`, then change the definition of `f` in the file, then re-import `temp`.

In that case, the second import will be a no-op and the first definition will be used.

**Case 3: `f` in a file and reloaded.**

Again the definition of `f` is in a file `temp.py`. Suppose we `import temp`, then change the definition of `f` in the file, then reload `temp`.

In that case, the reload causes the new definition to get re-executed and exported to the other machines, so the new definition should be used.

**Case 4: helper  function in a file.**

This is a bit more subtle. Suppose that `f` uses a helper function defined in a file. Then when `f` is defined, its definition is shipped to all of the workers, and unpickled. When it is unpickled, the file with the helper function is imported. Now suppose that the helper file is changed. If we redefine `f`, then the definition of `f` is pickled again and shipped to the workers. When it is unpickled on a worker, the worker imports the helper file, but since the helper file has been imported already, this import is a no-op, so the new definition of the helper function **is not used**. Reloading on the driver doesn't help because the reload needs to happen on the worker.

issue_title: Maybe non-conformant implementation: not treating data as a stream
issue_body:
For example, in the [_on_local_read](https://github.com/shadowsocks/shadowsocks/blob/master/shadowsocks/tcprelay.py#L536) function which handles the local SOCKS requests, shadowsocks reads from socket only once in each stage and do no completeness validation. 

We can easily produce a problem showing this issue: (OS: Ubuntu 16.04.2 LTS x86-64)

```python
# Python 3.5.2 (default, Nov 17 2016, 17:05:23) 
# [GCC 5.4.0 20160609] on linux
# Type "help", "copyright", "credits" or "license" for more information.
>>> import socket
>>> s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
>>> s.connect(('127.0.0.1', 1080))
>>> s.send(b'\x05\x01\x00') # Send the connection message
3
>>> s.recv(2) # Successfully received the METHOD selection message
b'\x05\x00'
>>> s.send(b'\x05') # Send the VER part of the request message only
1
```

Apparently, this is not acceptable according to the current shadowsocks implementation. It is assumed that the complete message is received, but the further resolving of the message results in an 'index out of range':

```
2017-02-23 06:14:50 ERROR    index out of range
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "shadowsocks/../shadowsocks/shell.py", line 88, in wrapper
    func(self, *args, **kwargs)
  File "shadowsocks/../shadowsocks/tcprelay.py", line 296, in _handle_stage_addr
    cmd = common.ord(data[1])
IndexError: index out of range
2017-02-23 06:14:50 ERROR    index out of range when handling connection from 127.0.0.1:60494
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "shadowsocks/../shadowsocks/shell.py", line 88, in wrapper
    func(self, *args, **kwargs)
  File "shadowsocks/../shadowsocks/tcprelay.py", line 296, in _handle_stage_addr
    cmd = common.ord(data[1])
IndexError: index out of range
```

I do not think that it conforms the specification. But happy to listen if I am wrong :)

This issue exists in both python and libev versions, but is not reproducable on macOS. I don't know why, though.

issue_title: Activation token invalidated by user login
issue_body:
Hello, thanks for the great work on this library!

`ActivationView` uses Django's `PasswordResetTokenGenerator` to generate the activation token. To create this token, this class hashes internal user state, including the user's last login. What this means is that if the user logs in before clicking the activation link (in situations where the activation link is sent, but not required), the token is invalidated and will not work. The API returns a 400 with  `INVALID_TOKEN_ERROR`. 

This token hashing behavior makes sense for password reset tokens, which is what the Django utility was designed for. However when creating activation tokens we should not hash the user's last login.

What are your thoughts on this? Would you be willing to accept a pull request?

issue_title: version.py on master is wrong
issue_body:
```diff
--- version.py	(revision d83191da508a9ebad66f497c76471a6892440bdf)
+++ version.py	(revision )
@@ -12,4 +12,4 @@
 # See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
 # limitations under the License.
 
-version = '1.0.3a1'
+version = '1.1.0a1'
```

After a release is branched off, master's minor version (the second number in a version string) should be incremented. On a release branch, only the hotfix (the 3rd number) is incremented after a releases.

```

1--2--3 master branch
    \
     4--5 releases/1.0.x branch
     ^
     |
     +--- releases/1.0.0 tag
```

Commits 1 and 2 are general commits, most likely merge commits that integrate features or bug fixes into the master branch before the release. They will have `version=1.0.0a1` in version.py or setup.py.

Commit 3 changes version.py from `1.0.0a1` to `1.1.0a1`.

Commit 4 changes version.py from `1.0.0a1` to `1.0.0`.

Commit 5 changes version.py `1.0.0` to `1.0.1a1`.

issue_title: Backend process negotiated information on crash / restart
issue_body:
ExaBGP currently can send OPEN, then UPDATE message to the backend process. The OPEN information is required to be able to decode UPDATE ( ASN4, and ADD-PATH negotiation ).

Should the process crash, the process need to have persistently store this data otherwise parsing is impossible.

ExaBGP could help by (both or either):
 - allow the backend process to re-request both send/received OPEN
 - include the OPEN negotiation information in each UPDATE
